  - Marketplace security overview (public)
  - Data handling & retention (public-ish)
  - Audit logs (public-ish)
  - Evidence (gated / on request)

Thoughts?

Naomi Feldman: security pages: we can publish a “marketplace security overview” + shared responsibility model + high-level encryption/keys. BUT evidence links should NOT be public. we can point to “SOC 2 Type II available under NDA / via trust portal” wording.

Marcus Lin: +1. also be careful with “supports” vs “enforces” language on retention (configurable vs default). pls link to the retention doc but keep it explicit what’s default and what’s configurable.

Tessa Morgan: UX note: landing page should visually mirror console screens (Subscribe → Verify entitlement → Create/link org → Create API key → First request). even if docs are text-heavy, a simple diagram helps.

Jean-Paul Laurent: if we put “Marketplace” under Getting started, can we also add a callout on the generic “Quickstart” pages that says “Bought via AWS/GCP marketplace? start here” w/ link? otherwise people will follow old quickstart and then get confused.

Alex Martinez: yes, we should do a small “marketplace entrypoint” banner on quickstarts + on pricing page.

Wei Chen: for Hosted path, AWS/GCP guides should share 80% of the same steps. can we avoid duplicate content by having one “marketplace flow” page and then AWS/GCP pages that are mostly provider-specific bits (subscription screenshot + entitlement timing + where to find IDs)?

Elliot Price: we can do:
- /marketplace (overview + common flow)
- /marketplace/aws (AWS-specific: subscribe UI, customer identifier, private offer activation, entitlement propagation notes)
- /marketplace/gcp (GCP-specific: procurement steps, billing account selection, etc)
- /marketplace/troubleshooting (shared)

Kira Thompson: pls include “what plan did I buy?” section somewhere. example: Dedicated buyers need to know they still need to talk to SE to provision capacity / region + that marketplace subscription is the procurement layer, not the infra magically appearing.

Ben Carter: +1 but keep it crisp. maybe a “Choose your deployment” section with 3 tiles: Hosted / Dedicated / Private. each tile shows: what you get immediately vs what requires coordination.

Naomi Feldman: also link security overview from the landing page, but don’t make onboarding block on it. procurement folks will go hunting though—so prominent placement is good.

Marcus Lin: troubleshooting page should include entitlement timing expectations (e.g., “can take up to X minutes”), retry guidance, and what info to include in a support ticket (org id, marketplace provider, subscription id, region). keep it practical.

Alex Martinez: we should standardize the URLs we share with partners:
1) onboarding landing
2) security overview
3) troubleshooting
That’s what AWS asked for last time.

Elliot Price: what do we call the nav item? “Marketplace onboarding” vs just “Marketplace”? (trying not to bury it)

Wei Chen: nav label “Marketplace” is fine. H1 on landing can be “Marketplace onboarding (AWS & GCP)”.

Tessa Morgan: agree. “Marketplace” in nav is easy to scan.

Kira Thompson: for troubleshooting, can we split sections by symptom?
- I subscribed but Redwood says no entitlement
- I can’t link to an existing org
- I created an org but no API key page / permissions
- Billing dimension mismatch / unexpected line items

Ben Carter: yes please. and add “still stuck? contact support” with clear routing. marketplace customers always ask “AWS or Redwood?”

Naomi Feldman: + make sure support routing doesn’t imply AWS has access to our systems. “Contact Redwood support; AWS/GCP can only help with marketplace purchase mechanics.”

Marcus Lin: also add an “Incident/status” link if we have one. entitlement sync job issues will look like user error.

Alex Martinez: URLs / redirects: we have an internal request in flight for vanity URLs. i’d like to keep these stable:
- /docs/marketplace
- /docs/marketplace/aws
- /docs/marketplace/gcp
- /docs/marketplace/troubleshooting
- /docs/security/marketplace
If we need legacy redirects from old marketplace pages, now’s the time.

Elliot Price: ok. action items:
- Elliot: update IA in docs repo + nav + stubs with final URLs (today)
- Wei/Kira: confirm content owners per page (Hosted bits vs Dedicated/Private bits) (tomorrow)
- Naomi/Marcus: review wording for security links + retention/audit log claims (this week)
- Alex: coordinate redirects/vanity URL ticket + confirm with Partnerships which exact links go into AWS/GCP submission forms

Ben Carter: pls add a short “What happens after you subscribe?” section that includes entitlement delay expectations + a link to troubleshooting. reduces support load.

Elliot Price: :thumbsup: will do. I’ll post the updated sitemap once the PR is up.

Alex Martinez: thanks. once PR is live, i’ll share with Partnerships + PM for sign-off.

Marcus Lin: ping me on the PR. I want to double check we’re not over-promising on retention defaults.

Naomi Feldman: same. also let’s keep evidence references as “available upon request” and link to the security overview page rather than a dead-end.

Wei Chen: cool. i’ll start consolidating the common flow content so AWS/GCP pages are genuinely provider-specific.

Kira Thompson: and I’ll add the Dedicated/Private callouts + “what you get immediately” section.

Elliot Price: perfect. closing loop: nav = Getting started → Marketplace; Security section includes Marketplace security overview; troubleshooting is a first-class page under Marketplace. done.

source.body:
Elliot Price: Quick q for @Naomi Feldman / @Dr. Aisha Rahman / @Olga Petrov — for the marketplace security packet, what is OK to publish publicly on docs vs what needs to be gated (sales-assisted / NDA / on-request)? We need to lock this today so we can finalize IA + URLs for AWS/GCP forms.

Alex Martinez: +1. AWS asked for “security overview” link that a reviewer can open without emailing us. GCP similar. But we don’t want to accidentally publish anything we normally keep in Trust Center.

Naomi Feldman: Can we list the specific sections you’re thinking about? Some content is fine public (high-level), some should definitely be gated (SOC2 report, pen test).

Elliot Price: Draft sections (from the Confluence index):
1) Security overview + shared responsibility
2) Data flow diagram
3) Encryption (in transit/at rest), KMS options
4) Audit logs (what events, export)
5) Data retention/residency
6) Subprocessors
7) SOC2 / ISO posture + evidence pointers
8) Vulnerability mgmt / pen tests

Wei Chen: Also there’s copy in the listing that currently says “SOC2 + ISO compliant” (from an older block). I’m worried that’s not precise.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Great flag. We should avoid “ISO compliant” unless we’re certified and can substantiate. Use “SOC 2 Type II” if accurate, and for ISO say “ISO 27001-aligned controls” or “ISO 27001 program in progress” (whatever is true).

Olga Petrov: For clarity: we have SOC 2 Type II report for the current period (available under NDA). We are not publicly posting the full report. ISO 27001: we’re aligned and in audit prep, but not certified yet.

Marcus Lin: +1 on gating anything that reveals control implementation details (e.g., specific tools/vendors in depth, network diagrams with too much detail, test results).

Kira Thompson: What about the data flow diagram? The draft is pretty high-level (Request -> Gateway -> Router -> Inference Runtime -> Logging/Metrics). No IPs/regions listed.

Naomi Feldman: High-level data flow is fine public if it’s abstract (boxes + arrows) and doesn’t enumerate internal service names, cloud account IDs, or exact infra topology.

Alex Martinez: AWS marketplace review explicitly asked for “architecture diagram + data flow” in the call notes. If we gate that, it’ll slow approval.

Sofia Mendes: Looping in quickly — from legal perspective: public is OK for general security statements if they are accurate, non-misleading, and don’t create contractual commitments. Avoid absolutes like “always,” “guaranteed,” “zero access.” Prefer “designed to,” “we implement,” “by default,” “customers can configure.”

Camila Reyes: Marketing side: we also don’t want a doc that reads like a compliance report. Needs to be buyer-friendly. But agreed, we should provide something linkable for marketplace.

Elliot Price: Proposed split:
PUBLIC docs page: Security Overview for Marketplace (includes 1-6 at high level)
GATED/On-request: SOC2 report, bridge letter, pen test summary, vuln scan outputs, detailed policies
Does that align?

Olga Petrov: Yes. Also: subprocessor list can be public, but keep it to the standard “Subprocessors” page we already maintain (names + purpose + location). Don’t include internal DPAs or MSAs.

Wei Chen: For SOC2/ISO section on the public page, can we say:
- “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report (available under NDA upon request).”
- “Redwood’s security program is aligned to ISO 27001 control domains.”
And link to “Request security documents” form.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That wording is acceptable. Add timeframe language carefully (e.g., “current SOC 2 Type II report”) but don’t mention audit firm name publicly.

Marcus Lin: On encryption: OK to state “TLS 1.2+ in transit” and “AES-256 at rest” if that’s accurate across all storage types (object store, DB, logs). If there are exceptions, qualify.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid enumerating exact cipher suites. Keep it “industry-standard encryption in transit (TLS)” and “encryption at rest” + “customer-managed keys available for Private / Dedicated (where applicable).” We should be explicit which deployment modes support CMK.

Kira Thompson: For retention/residency: we can publish defaults + what’s configurable (e.g., request logs retention window, prompt/response logging toggles), but avoid stating exact numbers that might change without review.

Alex Martinez: Our retention doc currently has exact defaults (e.g., “X days”). That’s been useful for procurement.

Sofia Mendes: If you publish exact retention durations, treat them as product promises. If we do, we need a change management process + review when defaults change.

Olga Petrov: Suggest: public page says “By default, metadata and operational logs are retained for a limited period to support reliability and abuse prevention” + “customers can configure retention in Dedicated/Private; Hosted has fixed defaults.” Then have a gated appendix with exact numbers (or in the Trust Center).

Elliot Price: That’s doable. But marketplace reviewers sometimes want the actual number.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Compromise: put exact numbers in a “Marketplace Security Addendum (Customer-Facing)” PDF that is accessible after lightweight gating (e.g., email capture + click-through) OR “available to verified marketplace reviewers upon request.” AWS reviewers will email if they need it. We should not post sensitive retention specifics without a control process.

Alex Martinez: For onboarding friction: if AWS wants a URL in the submission, we can put the public overview URL + a “request docs” URL. That might pass.

Marcus Lin: Audit logs section: OK to list event categories (org changes, API key events, SSO config, model deployment changes, admin actions) and export destinations (S3, GCS, SIEM via webhook) without sharing schemas.

Wei Chen: Also, do we include “We don’t train on customer data” on the public page? That’s asked constantly.

Sofia Mendes: Be careful: depends on definitions. Safer: “Customer prompts and outputs are not used to train Redwood-hosted foundation models by default.” If there are exceptions (explicit opt-in), state that.

Naomi Feldman: +1. And clarify: logs may be used for abuse detection and service improvement (aggregated/limited), but not for model training absent opt-in.

Camila Reyes: Please avoid heavy hedging that reads like “we might do anything.” Buyers want clarity. Maybe: “We do not use customer content to train models unless the customer explicitly opts in.”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s fine if it’s accurate. Also note that for Private deployments, customer controls the environment and can enforce stricter controls.

Elliot Price: Action items:
- @Elliot Price to draft PUBLIC “Marketplace Security Overview” page with: shared responsibility, high-level data flow, encryption, audit logs, subprocessors link, high-level retention/residency, and a “Request security docs” CTA.
- @Naomi Feldman / @Marcus Lin to review encryption + audit logs wording for accuracy.
- @Olga Petrov to provide approved SOC2/ISO posture language + what we can/can’t say publicly.
- @Sofia Mendes to review for legal sensitivity + avoid contractual commitments.

Alex Martinez: Can we also align on a single sentence for marketplace listing copy re: compliance? We keep getting comments back from AWS on over-claiming.

Olga Petrov: Suggested: “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report and follows a risk-based security program aligned to ISO 27001 domains. Compliance evidence is available upon request.”

Wei Chen: :thumbsup: I’ll update the copy block to that.

Marcus Lin: One more: don’t publish the full incident response policy publicly. But we can say we have 24/7 monitoring + documented IR process.

Naomi Feldman: Agree. Also no details on red team frequency, external vendor names, or exact SLA response times unless those are in contracts.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll post a draft link in this thread tomorrow for review.

Camila Reyes: Thanks. Once we have the public page, we’ll use it for the marketplace listing “Security” section and for procurement emails.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please add a small “Last updated” line and an email for reporting vulnerabilities (security@redwoodinference.com) on the public page.

Elliot Price: Will do.

source.content:
Elliot Price: Drafting the FAQ for tiered batching. Need crisp answer for: “What happens if I don’t pick a tier?” Also need an example showing override mechanisms without sounding like it’s auto-tuning.

Wei Chen: +1. For Hosted docs I’d like: default behavior, how to set per request (SDK/header), and how to set per route in Console.

Kira Thompson: And a Dedicated callout: tenant override precedence. We’ve already had confusion in other features when people set route policy but Dedicated override wins (or vice versa).

Elliot Price: What’s the intended default? “Standard” tier?

Nikhil Sharma: For Hosted: yes, default tier is Standard unless you explicitly set Fast or Cost-efficient.

Henry Cho: We should be careful with wording: we are workload-aware *within* the tier (prompt length buckets, streaming flag) but we aren’t automatically switching customers between tiers unless they configure a policy.

Elliot Price: Proposed FAQ text (WIP):
- If you don’t choose a tier, Redwood uses Standard tier defaults.
- Standard is designed for balanced latency/cost for most workloads.
- You can override per route, or per request (if enabled), and Dedicated tenants can set a cluster/tenant default.

Sofia Ivanova: Yep. Also mention: if you set nothing, we still may enter “overload protection mode” (backpressure / shedding) to keep things stable; it’s not “we’ll just keep batching harder”.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to overload note. In FAQ, maybe: “Under sustained overload, Redwood may apply backpressure (429/503) rather than letting queueing blow up p99.” Avoid too many implementation details.

Wei Chen: For “override mechanisms” can we list the exact knobs customers see? I’m seeing SDK PR adds `batching_tier` param and there’s also a header.

Henry Cho: The current UX is:
1) Route-level policy in Console / config: `batching_tier: fast|standard|cost_efficient`
2) Optional request override via header for API/SDK (we should document it as “advanced”, and note it might be disabled by org policy)
3) Dedicated: tenant/cluster override (admin-only)
Precedence should be consistent in docs.

Kira Thompson: What’s the actual precedence? I want one canonical list.

Sofia Ivanova: From implementation notes: explicit customer override > route policy > tier default. For Dedicated, tenant override counts as explicit customer override.

Nikhil Sharma: For Hosted, request override (header/SDK) is the most specific, then route policy. If neither is set, Standard tier.

Elliot Price: Ok, so in docs we can say:
- Most specific wins: request override (if allowed) → route setting → account/tenant default (Dedicated) → platform default (Standard).
Is that accurate?

Kira Thompson: Slight tweak: Dedicated tenant default should probably be evaluated before route policy or after?

Sofia Ivanova: After some debate we landed on: request override > Dedicated tenant override > route policy > platform default. Reason: Dedicated tenant override is the “safety rail” for that cluster.

Kira Thompson: Great. That matches the Dedicated requirement thread.

Wei Chen: Can we add: “Console shows the effective tier and why” (reason codes). That helps support.

Elliot Price: Yep. I’ll add a line: “You can view the effective tier and selection reason in Console → Route details → Batching.”

Sean Gallagher: Please avoid promising “always visible” on day 1. Say “where available” or “in supported regions/versions” since rollout is staged.

Elliot Price: Noted.

Henry Cho: For the FAQ answer, I’d also include: “If you don’t pick a tier, you’ll get the same behavior as Standard going forward; you don’t need to migrate anything unless you had custom batching knobs.”

Wei Chen: I’m going to paste a draft snippet for Hosted docs:

Wei Chen: 
FAQ: What happens if I don’t pick a batching tier?
- If you don’t set a tier, Redwood applies the **Standard** batching defaults for the model you’re using.
- Standard is intended to balance latency and cost for typical production traffic.
- Redwood may adjust internal batching behavior based on workload signals (e.g., prompt length distribution, streaming vs. non-streaming) while staying within the Standard tier’s latency targets.
- Under overload, Redwood prioritizes stability (backpressure / load shedding) over unbounded queueing.

Elliot Price: Looks good. I’ll tweak the “latency targets” language so it doesn’t sound like an SLA for everyone.

Nikhil Sharma: +1. Maybe: “designed to meet published targets” instead of “staying within”.

Kira Thompson: Also add a mini table (one-liner):
Fast = tight tail latency, lower batching
Standard = balanced
Cost-efficient = higher batching/throughput, may increase tail latency

Elliot Price: I’ll add that as a callout card.

Wei Chen: Need an example of override mechanisms. Something like:

```bash
curl https://api.redwood.example/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -H "X-Redwood-Batching-Tier: fast" \
  -d '{"model":"redwood/llama-3.1-70b","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"..."}]}'
```

And SDK:

```python
client.chat.completions.create(
  model="redwood/llama-3.1-70b",
  messages=[...],
  batching_tier="cost_efficient",
)
```

Elliot Price: I like it, but we should add a note: header override may be disabled for some orgs, and route-level setting is recommended.

Henry Cho: Yes. Also we should normalize naming: docs should use “cost-efficient” for the tier name, but code uses `cost_efficient`. Suggest: “Use `cost_efficient` in API/SDK.”

Wei Chen: I’ll add a parenthetical: “API value: `cost_efficient`”.

Sean Gallagher: Also add troubleshooting: “If you set a tier but don’t see a change, check whether your org/tenant policy restricts request-level overrides; check Console effective tier.” That will deflect a bunch of tickets.

Elliot Price: Good call. Adding to FAQ:
- “Why isn’t my tier setting taking effect?”
  - Request override disabled by admin
  - Route policy overrides request (if we ever do that)
  - Dedicated tenant override forcing a tier
  - Not yet rolled out to your runtime version/region

Kira Thompson: Dedicated bullet should be explicit: “On Dedicated, a tenant/cluster default may override route settings.”

Sofia Ivanova: And we should mention: if model has constraints (e.g., quantization/profile compatibility), platform may fall back to the closest compatible profile but keep the same tier label. (This is where “reason codes” help.)

Elliot Price: Hmm, that’s subtle. For public docs, maybe: “In rare cases, the platform may adjust the underlying batching profile to maintain correctness/compatibility.”

Nikhil Sharma: That’s fine. Don’t mention quantization here; keep it batching-focused.

Wei Chen: Action items:
- Elliot: update FAQ text + add override examples + troubleshooting
- Wei: ensure terminology matches SDK (`batching_tier`, header name)
- Kira: add Dedicated precedence paragraph + link to Dedicated override doc section

Elliot Price: Done. I’ll post the final copy here before opening the docs PR.

Elliot Price: Updated draft (for review):

“**What happens if I don’t pick a batching tier?**
If you don’t set a batching tier, Redwood applies the **Standard** tier by default. Standard is designed to provide a balanced trade-off between latency and cost for most workloads. Redwood may adjust internal batching behavior based on workload signals (such as prompt length and whether responses are streamed) while aiming for Standard’s published performance targets.

If the system is overloaded, Redwood prioritizes stability. You may see backpressure (e.g., retries or rate limiting) rather than unbounded queueing that can cause extreme tail latency.

**How do I set or override the tier?**
You can set the tier at the route level (recommended) or, where enabled, per request.
- Per request (advanced): `X-Redwood-Batching-Tier: fast|standard|cost_efficient` (API value for cost-efficient is `cost_efficient`)
- Per route: configure the batching tier in Console or your route policy config
- Dedicated: an admin can set a tenant/cluster default tier

**Precedence (most specific wins):** request override (if allowed) → Dedicated tenant/cluster override → route policy → Standard default.

**How can I confirm what’s active?**
In Console, check the route’s effective tier (and, where available, the selection reason) to verify which setting is in effect.”

Henry Cho: Looks good. One tiny wording tweak: “aiming for published performance targets” is ok.

Kira Thompson: Precedence line is perfect. Please add a sentence: “Dedicated overrides are intended as guardrails for shared cluster behavior.”

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add: “If you suspect a regression, switch back to Standard and contact support with request IDs.”

Wei Chen: Approved from Hosted docs side. I’ll sync with the SDK docs once PR lands. :thumbsup:

Elliot Price: hey all — quick docs/devex q on the standard cost model work. Where do we want to document field semantics (prefill/decode, cache hit, batch efficiency, overhead allocation, etc.) so we don’t end up with Console docs saying one thing and Optimize/billing saying another?

Elliot Price: also: how do we keep whatever we publish in sync w/ the schema package + codegen? I’ve seen this drift on other APIs.

Ethan Park: +1. we have (a) confluence spec, (b) schema package in monorepo, (c) internal docs site page for the API usage. need a single “source of truth” for field semantics.

Chloe Martin: Console needs copy we can link to from the UI tooltips. ideally stable URLs + versioned definitions. also we need to explain why a number might differ from invoice if there’s overhead vs request-attributable.

Aditya Rao: Optimize is mostly about “what-if” semantics (what’s held constant, what changes) + confidence intervals. we can’t have those living only in a confluence page that gets stale.

Nadia Rahman: from telemetry side we need very crisp units + attribution rules. e.g. prefill_tokens vs decode_tokens are *counted* not “estimated”; cache hit rate is defined on kv cache blocks not prompts; ppl get confused.

Allison Grant: suggestion: treat the schema package as the normative contract. everything else should be generated or a thin narrative wrapper that links back to the schema.

Benji Okafor: what format is the schema package? protobuf? jsonschema? typescript structs?

Ethan Park: v1 is a versioned “cost-model-schema” package in the monorepo. currently: protobuf-ish IDL + generated TS/Go types + some markdown. we can add per-field comments/annotations.

Elliot Price: ok so proposal could be:
1) authoritative definitions live as field-level docstrings/annotations in the schema package
2) internal docs site renders those into a reference page (per version)
3) confluence stays high-level (overview + ADR links), but not the place for enumerating every field

Elliot Price: does that match what folks want?

Jada Williams: +1. confluence is great for “why/intent”, but for implementation details, devs should always end up at the schema reference.

Marissa Cole: for Console, we also need “how to explain this to a user” language. maybe that’s separate from the canonical field definition? i don’t want product copy to change the meaning.

Allison Grant: yep, split it:
- Canonical semantics (normative): schema docs
- Product explanation (non-normative): Console/Optimize UX docs, but must link to canonical and not redefine terms

Chloe Martin: can we add a short “interpretation guidance” section next to each major block? like overhead_allocation.* fields: what they mean, typical ranges, why it’s there.

Elliot Price: we can, but I’d keep it still attached to schema version. otherwise we’ll publish guidance for v1 and then v1.1 changes behavior.

Nadia Rahman: also please document what fields are optional vs required and under what conditions. e.g. kv_cache.* may be missing for older runtime versions or private deployments w/ different instrumentation.

Ethan Park: we already have “presence conditions” as part of the design spec. we can encode that as annotations too (like `availability: runtime>=X` / `deployment_modes: hosted|dedicated|private`).

Benji Okafor: if we do annotations, can we enforce in CI that docs exist for every exported field? otherwise ppl add a field and forget to update docs.

Elliot Price: yes. we can add a docs-lint check:
- fails if any public field lacks a description
- fails if enums lack per-value descriptions
- optionally: fails if “units” missing for numeric fields

Allison Grant: + add a “breaking change guard” that checks docstrings for deprecation tags. :thumbsup:

Aditya Rao: for what-if: we need a place to document modeling assumptions. e.g. when user asks “increase batch size”, what happens to queue time? do we assume constant latency SLO or constant throughput? That’s not purely a field description.

Ethan Park: I think that belongs in endpoint-level docs (still generated from the API IDL). field docs cover “what is `projected_cost.usd`”, endpoint docs cover “how we compute projection + what’s held constant”.

Elliot Price: makes sense. We can structure docs site as:
- /cost-model-api/v1/overview
- /cost-model-api/v1/endpoints (with assumptions + examples)
- /cost-model-schema/v1/reference (field-by-field)

Chloe Martin: and then Console UI links to /cost-model-schema/v1/reference#cost_breakdown.overhead.* or similar.

Jada Williams: pls make sure anchors are stable. we’ve had doc generators change anchor names.

Elliot Price: yep. we can generate anchors based on fully-qualified field paths, not headings that change.

Nadia Rahman: another gotcha: people use “overhead” colloquially. the taxonomy ADR has exact categories. we should link ADR-022 from the overhead section.

Elliot Price: will do.

Benji Okafor: where does versioning show up? if Console calls schema v1.0 but docs site shows latest v1.x, that can still confuse.

Ethan Park: API responses include `schema_version`. client negotiates. so docs should be per version, and “latest” should redirect but also show “you’re viewing v1.2”.

Elliot Price: ack. I’ll coordinate with DevEx to ensure docs build publishes per tag/release of schema package.

Marissa Cole: can we also include a short “billing vs cost model” note somewhere? support is already getting questions on mismatch.

Ethan Park: yes but careful w/ wording. cost model is for observability/optimization + finance reporting primitives; billing can apply contract/pricing adjustments.

Allison Grant: recommend a standard disclaimer block that is reused across pages (so not rewritten by every team). “Not an invoice. For billing refer to …”

Aditya Rao: + a note on confidence intervals: when Optimize shows a savings range, it’s from `projection.confidence_interval` and it has semantics (statistical vs heuristic). we should define that.

Elliot Price: action items:
- [Elliot] draft docs structure proposal + doc generation plan (schema annotations -> reference site)
- [Ethan] confirm schema package supports needed annotations (units, availability, deprecation)
- [Chloe/Marissa] send list of UI tooltip terms that must map 1:1 to canonical fields
- [Aditya] provide what-if assumptions list (endpoint-level docs)

Elliot Price: I’ll post a short writeup in confluence too but will keep it linking out, not duplicating.

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will send tooltip list.

Aditya Rao: +1 I’ll share the what-if assumptions we’ve been using in the ROI scratchpad (and what we’re comfortable committing to for v1).

Nadia Rahman: pls include examples w/ units. eg cache_hit_rate is 0..1, not percent; prefill_tokens is integer count; costs are in USD microdollars or decimals?

Ethan Park: costs are decimal USD in the API, but internal primitives are micros. we should document both in schema + endpoint docs.

Elliot Price: got it. also adding docs-lint to CI so we don’t ship fields w/out semantics. will follow up w/ PR once I coordinate w/ release train.

Allison Grant: cc me on the docs-lint proposal, I can sanity check against API standards addendum.

Elliot Price: will do. thanks all.

source.thread:
Elliot Price: Dropping the outline for the Shadow Traffic + Quality Metrics docs quickstart here for review. Goal is “enable safely + interpret deltas” in <10 min. Draft outline (WIP):
1) What is shadow traffic? (and what it is NOT)
2) When to use it (rollouts/canaries) + common pitfalls
3) Quickstart (Hosted + Dedicated)
4) Metrics: what we ship in v1 + how scoring works at a high level
5) Interpreting deltas (CIs, sample size)
6) Safety / overhead / guardrails (fail-open)
7) Data retention + redaction
8) APIs/SDK (create experiment, query aggregates, export examples)
9) FAQ (streaming, tool calls, private)

If folks can call out missing warnings / examples, that’d be great. Link to doc draft in PR: https://github.com/redwood-docs/pull/511 (still private)

Dylan Brooks: Thanks. Big +1 on “what it is NOT” up top. We should explicitly say: “does not change user response; control variant still serves the response.” People keep asking if it’s an A/B.

Dylan Brooks: Also recommend a callout near quickstart: shadow results are async; dashboards may lag (and that’s expected). We had dogfood confusion where folks thought “missing data == broken.”

Allison Grant: +1. Can we add a small “data freshness” box:
- ingestion delay target (e.g., p50 ~ few min, p95 maybe 15-30 depending on scoring)
- what to check if it’s >1h (link to runbook later)

Elliot Price: Yep. I can add a “When do results show up?” callout + troubleshooting section.

Ravi Menon: Routing-specific warning: emphasize fail-open + timeouts. Shadow mirroring happens on a separate path; if shadow target errors/timeouts, it should not impact control. But customers need to understand they’ll see “shadow error rate” in the experiment.

Ravi Menon: Suggest an explicit snippet of the mirror behavior:
- Request comes in
- Control responds
- Shadow request is mirrored with strict timeout
- Shadow response captured (partial for streaming)

Kenji Nakamura: From UX perspective: we should reuse the same language as Console tooltips.
- “Shadow = duplicate sampled requests to candidates, evaluate asynchronously”
- “Candidate output is never returned to end users”
If docs differs we’ll get support pings.

Monica Patel: For SDK/examples, we should include at least one end-to-end:
- create shadow experiment
- poll aggregates
- fetch example diffs
In python + TS would be ideal, but if only one language, python please.

Elliot Price: Noted. Current plan: python + curl. We can add TS in a follow-up page. (Monica, if you have a draft snippet from the SDK PR, drop it here?)

Monica Patel: Here’s what’s in the python SDK (rough):
```py
from redwood import Redwood
client = Redwood(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"])

exp = client.shadow_experiments.create(
  route_id="rt_7f2...",
  control={"model": "rw/llama-3.1-70b", "revision": "2026-01-12"},
  candidates=[{"name": "q4_kv", "model": "rw/llama-3.1-70b", "quant": "int4"}],
  sampling={"rate": 0.01, "bucket_key": "stable"},
  metrics_bundle="quality-v1",
  retention_days=3,
)

summary = client.shadow_experiments.aggregates(exp.id, window="6h")
examples = client.shadow_experiments.examples(exp.id, limit=20, filter="regressions_only")
```
Caveat: field names might shift (we’re still aligning on “candidates” vs “variants”).

Dylan Brooks: Re: examples: be careful, we shouldn’t promise “diffs” always. Sometimes we only store redacted/truncated prompts. So docs should say “sampled redacted examples (if enabled by policy).”

Marcus Lin: Please make the data handling section very explicit:
- Default is minimal retention; prompt/response content may be truncated/redacted.
- Customers can opt out of storing raw content entirely (still get aggregate metrics).
- Access controls: example-level content is restricted (RBAC) + audit logged.
Also mention: do NOT paste secrets into prompts; Redwood will attempt redaction but it’s not a DLP product.

Kevin Osei: +1. Also needs a clear statement on Dedicated/Private knobs:
- Dedicated: retention + redaction configurable per tenant; default short.
- Private: storage stays in customer env; can export to SIEM; air-gapped scoring is optional / may limit model-graded metrics.

Sean Gallagher: Can we add an “overhead budget” callout? The biggest operational risk is customers cranking sampling to 50% and then complaining about cost.
Something like:
- Start at 0.5–1% sampling
- Increase only when you need power
- Shadow compute/scoring is billed separately (if applicable) / impacts capacity

Marissa Cole: Product asks: include a “recommended workflow” section:
1) Start with shadow on candidate during canary
2) Watch quality deltas + latency/cost
3) If quality OK + performance OK, then proceed to rollout
4) If quality regresses, rollback candidate (advisory at launch)
Also call out that “automatic rollback on quality” is not default in beta.

Elliot Price: Great. I’ll add a section “Using shadow with rollouts” and be explicit about advisory vs automatic.

Allison Grant: Observability nit: please avoid encouraging high-cardinality filtering in examples (e.g., “filter by user_id”). We need to steer people toward safe dimensions.
Maybe include a list “supported breakdowns” (route, region, model, variant, request_type) and say “custom tags may be bucketed/dropped.”

Ravi Menon: +1. Also mention deterministic bucketing briefly. Folks will ask why the candidate sample doesn’t match exactly hour-to-hour if they change the policy.

Elliot Price: For bucketing: I can do a short note:
- stable request fingerprint
- consistent hashing into buckets
- changing bucket_key or sampling rate will change membership

Dylan Brooks: Streaming edge case needs a dedicated FAQ answer.
We should state:
- For streaming, we may stop capturing after N tokens / timeout
- Some metrics are computed on partial outputs and labeled as such
- “format adherence” can be misleading if output is truncated

Marcus Lin: Add “PII redaction” specifics at a high level (don’t list regexes, but describe behavior): allowlist mode for structured fields; hashing identifiers; prompt truncation.
And emphasize: candidates see the same redacted prompt content as stored (don’t imply we send redacted to candidate; we mirror the request; storage redaction happens in telemetry pipeline).

Elliot Price: Good catch. I’ll phrase it as “redaction applies to stored artifacts and example views; request mirroring follows your route payload.”

Kevin Osei: Also add: “If you need field-level suppression before the shadow target, use request transforms / header stripping in the policy.” (We have that in config.)

Monica Patel: For the quickstart, can we include a concrete curl for enabling the policy on a route? Something like:
```bash
curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/routes/rt_.../shadow_policy \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -d '{"enabled": true, "sampling_rate": 0.01, "targets": [{"name":"candidate", "model":"rw/..."}], "fail_open": true}'
```
Even if it’s pseudo, it helps.

Dylan Brooks: Please don’t invent endpoint paths if we’re not sure. Maybe reference the Console flow primarily and keep API example aligned with the API contract (ENG-5407). If we include curl, it must match the actual endpoint.

Elliot Price: Agreed. I’ll keep the curl to “create shadow experiment” against the public endpoint we’ve locked, and for enabling I’ll use Console screenshots + “policy attaches to route” conceptually.

Sean Gallagher: Docs should include “kill switch” guidance:
- set sampling to 0 or disable shadow policy
- what happens to queued scoring jobs (they’ll drain)
- where to look for circuit breaker status
Even if it’s just 3 bullets + link to runbook.

Allison Grant: And link to the dashboards names people should expect in Console (Quality Deltas, Scoring Lag, Shadow Target Errors). That saves on support.

Kenji Nakamura: I’ll paste the exact dashboard labels from the UI copy doc later today so docs matches.

Marissa Cole: Can we add one “interpretation” example with numbers? Like:
- Control rubric avg 0.82 vs candidate 0.79
- delta -0.03 with 95% CI [-0.06, +0.00]
- guidance: “not significant; increase sample or run longer”
And another where it’s clearly a regression.

Elliot Price: Yes. I’ll add two worked examples (one inconclusive, one regression) + a “don’t overreact to small deltas” note.

Marcus Lin: Final security note: add a warning about regulated workloads:
- If you’re in HIPAA/PCI/etc, confirm retention/redaction settings + data residency before enabling example capture.
- For Private, confirm where scoring runs (in-cluster vs external) before enabling model-graded metrics.

Elliot Price: Got it. Action items from this thread:
- [Elliot] Add strong “not A/B / no user impact” language + async freshness callout
- [Elliot] Add overhead/sampling guidance + kill switch section + streaming FAQ
- [Elliot] Add data retention/redaction + RBAC/audit log notes; clarify redaction applies to stored artifacts
- [Kenji] Provide exact UI labels / tooltip text to mirror
- [Monica] Confirm SDK field names (“candidates/variants”) before we publish snippets
- [Allison] Provide allowed breakdown dimensions list (cardinality-safe)

I’ll update the PR and re-request review tomorrow.

Kenji Nakamura: Will do. I’ll grab the latest copy strings from DES-613 notes and post.

Allison Grant: I’ll DM you the safe dimensions + the “do not include” list (user_id, prompt_id, request_id) so it’s crisp.

Monica Patel: I’ll confirm the API naming after today’s sync w/ Chloe/Jada and update snippet accordingly.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on this outline. Once the “kill switch + runbook link” is in, SRE is happy to sign off for beta.

Elliot Price: Perfect, thanks all.

source.text:
Ava Chen: opened PR to reconcile audit-log retention guidance for EU customers, add key-rotation notes, and migrate SDK examples to v3 surface. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/1107
Liam Ortiz: reading now. Two cross-cutting items: EU data locality wording and how we describe automatic key-rotation support.
Priya Reddy: for key rotation, we should show both server-side rotation (managed by KMS) and client-initiated rewrap flow. Customers asked for a small code example of rewrapping.
Karen Lee (Legal): legal check: avoid definitive legal language. Recommend: "Hosted default retention: 1 year. Customers may configure longer retention per contract or applicable law." Also: "This document is informational, not legal advice."
Ava Chen: acknowledged; will add the legal sentence. Also planning to rename examples folder `audit-exports/v3` and leave a short note about older v2 snippets being archived with links.
Liam Ortiz: snippet parity: the Python/Go/CLI shapes changed in SDK v3. Show both v3 and a v2->v3 mapping table for quick migration. Keep copy-paste runnable.
Priya Reddy: draft rewrap snippet (Python v3):
Priya Reddy: ```python
# Python SDK v3 - rewrap a stored envelope with a new CMK
from redwood_audit import AuditClient
client = AuditClient(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_KEY'))
client.rewrap_envelope(envelope_id='env_abc123', new_cmk='projects/PROJECT/locations/REGION/keyRings/NAME/cryptoKeys/KEY')
```
Ava Chen: replace provider path with the placeholder exactly as above. Add a short paragraph: "Rewrap operations require iam:encrypt/iam:decrypt on both keys."
Karen Lee (Legal): add a brief note about key-rotation notifications for customers who have contractual key-audit requirements.
docs-bot: build / docs:pr/1107: preview deployed at https://staging.docs.redwood.ai/security/audit-logs#migration-v3
Liam Ortiz: left one doc review comment about the migration table formatting. Other than that LGTM.
Ava Chen: merging once legal blurb added. Will include a redirect from `/audit-logs/v2` to `/audit-logs/v3/migration` and update changelog: "Audit-log: EU retention clarifications, key-rotation rewrap example, SDK v3 migration guide."
Priya Reddy: thanks all.
Karen Lee (Legal): legal review: 2026-03-23 by K. Lee - no objections.

text:
Ava Chen: opened PR to reconcile audit-log retention guidance for EU customers, add key-rotation notes, and migrate SDK examples to v3 surface. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/1107
Liam Ortiz: reading now. Two cross-cutting items: EU data locality wording and how we describe automatic key-rotation support.
Priya Reddy: for key rotation, we should show both server-side rotation (managed by KMS) and client-initiated rewrap flow. Customers asked for a small code example of rewrapping.
Karen Lee (Legal): legal check: avoid definitive legal language. Recommend: "Hosted default retention: 1 year. Customers may configure longer retention per contract or applicable law." Also: "This document is informational, not legal advice."
Ava Chen: acknowledged; will add the legal sentence. Also planning to rename examples folder `audit-exports/v3` and leave a short note about older v2 snippets being archived with links.
Liam Ortiz: snippet parity: the Python/Go/CLI shapes changed in SDK v3. Show both v3 and a v2->v3 mapping table for quick migration. Keep copy-paste runnable.
Priya Reddy: draft rewrap snippet (Python v3):
Priya Reddy: ```python
# Python SDK v3 - rewrap a stored envelope with a new CMK
from redwood_audit import AuditClient
client = AuditClient(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_KEY'))
client.rewrap_envelope(envelope_id='env_abc123', new_cmk='projects/PROJECT/locations/REGION/keyRings/NAME/cryptoKeys/KEY')
```
Ava Chen: replace provider path with the placeholder exactly as above. Add a short paragraph: "Rewrap operations require iam:encrypt/iam:decrypt on both keys."
Karen Lee (Legal): add a brief note about key-rotation notifications for customers who have contractual key-audit requirements.
docs-bot: build / docs:pr/1107: preview deployed at https://staging.docs.redwood.ai/security/audit-logs#migration-v3
Liam Ortiz: left one doc review comment about the migration table formatting. Other than that LGTM.
Ava Chen: merging once legal blurb added. Will include a redirect from `/audit-logs/v2` to `/audit-logs/v3/migration` and update changelog: "Audit-log: EU retention clarifications, key-rotation rewrap example, SDK v3 migration guide."
Priya Reddy: thanks all.
Karen Lee (Legal): legal review: 2026-03-23 by K. Lee - no objections.

source.content:
Elliot Price: hey folks — starting a thread to align on the Private docs updates for signed offline bundles. want to make sure we don’t ship docs that drift from the installer behavior.

Elliot Price: main asks from Docs/DevEx:
1) final CLI commands + expected output (verify + inspect)
2) screenshots/examples for Console vs installer (if any)
3) versioning story: what changes per release, how we keep it in sync

Kira Thompson: +1. I’m drafting the guide structure now:
- Overview (why signing, what’s in a bundle)
- Before you start (installer version requirement, trust store)
- Verify bundle (preflight + standalone)
- Install (air-gapped mode)
- Troubleshoot (common error codes)
- Key rotation / updating trust roots

Kira Thompson: need a canonical “happy path” snippet we can paste with minimal edits each release.

Ethan Park: installer side: preflight now blocks unless signature verifies + manifest hashes match. We also added `bundle inspect` command that prints a summary and can do `--verify`.

Ethan Park: current commands (subject to minor flag naming tweaks):
- `redwood-private-installer preflight --bundle /path/to/bundle.tar.zst --airgap`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --bundle /path/to/bundle.tar.zst --verify --output text`

Dylan Brooks: flags are stable as of the latest PR draft. only thing that might change is output formatting (we’re trying to make it deterministic for support copy/paste).

Kira Thompson: can someone paste a sample output for `bundle inspect --verify` that’s safe to publish? like w/ fake build ids.

Ethan Park: yup, here’s a representative output from staging (scrubbed). This matches what Support sees:
```
Bundle: redwood-private-offline-bundle_2026.01.0_build.6c2b1f1.tar.zst
Build ID: 6c2b1f1
Release: 2026.01.0
Manifest: bundle.manifest.json (sha256: 8a0b…)
Signature: bundle.manifest.sig (ed25519)
Signing key: RWDSIGN-2025Q4 (fingerprint: 4f:2d:…:a9)
Verification: OK
Artifacts:
  - helm/redwood-platform-2026.01.0.tgz (sha256: …)
  - images/index.json (sha256: …)
  - runtime/redwood-serving-2026.01.0+cuda12.tar (sha256: …)
SBOM:
  - sbom/spdx.json (sha256: …)
Notes:
  - Air-gapped mode: no network fetches will be attempted.
```

Elliot Price: perfect. we’ll include that as the “expected output” example.

Connor O'Brien: for keeping docs in sync w/ releases: release train will publish the bundle name + build id + signing key id in the release notes. docs should NOT hardcode key ids (those rotate), but can show the field and explain what it means.

Connor O'Brien: also: if docs mention “ed25519” explicitly, please caveat with “current signing algorithm” and point to “Signing details” section. We might swap envelope/alg later, and we don’t want public docs to promise specifics we can’t keep.

Kira Thompson: got it. I’ll phrase as “Redwood signs the manifest using an asymmetric signing key; installer verifies against trusted public keys”. then a callout: “As of 2026.01, signature type shown as ed25519”.

Sahana Iyer: +1 to not promising exact pipeline internals. but docs should be explicit on what verification checks:
- signature validates
- manifest canonicalization matches
- artifact hashes match
- downgrade protection (if present)

Sahana Iyer: customers ask “what exactly fails the install” — we should spell it out.

Dev Patel: Support request: please add a small table mapping error codes to next steps. We keep seeing people stuck on “UNKNOWN_KEY” vs “HASH_MISMATCH”.

Ethan Park: current taxonomy (names might be slightly different in the UI but conceptually):
- `SIG_INVALID`
- `TRUST_KEY_UNKNOWN`
- `MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH`
- `ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH`
- `SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID` (time skew)
- `SIGNATURE_EXPIRED`
- `DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED`
- `BREAK_GLASS_USED` (audit marker)

Elliot Price: awesome, we’ll incorporate. Dev, if you have the exact strings from your runbook / tickets, drop them here so docs match the copy.

Dev Patel: from last two cases:
- “untrusted signing key” (customer wording)
- “hash mismatch after transfer”
- “signature not yet valid”

Dev Patel: we don’t need to show every code, but the 5 common ones + remediation steps would be huge.

Kira Thompson: for screenshots: do we want to include any? installer is CLI only. For docs site we can include terminal blocks. screenshots are a pain to keep current.

Dylan Brooks: agree, prefer copy/paste blocks vs screenshots. if we do any images, it should be “bundle layout” diagram (static) not terminal output.

Elliot Price: +1. We’ll avoid screenshots unless Product insists. terminal snippets + callouts should be enough.

Logan Wright: reminder: verification events emit local audit logs in Private. docs should mention where those logs live and that they’re local-only by default (air-gapped). don’t promise any outbound telemetry.

Kira Thompson: can you share the default log path + how to export?

Logan Wright: defaults (Linux):
- installer logs: `/var/log/redwood/installer/installer.log`
- audit events: `/var/log/redwood/audit/audit.log`
Export depends on customer; if they run our audit-log-exporter it can forward to SIEM. But in true air-gap, they’ll pull files manually.

Elliot Price: we’ll add “Audit logging” section:
- what gets logged on verify success/failure
- redaction (no artifact bytes; only ids/hashes)
- retention guidance (suggest 30–90d)

Connor O'Brien: docs sync plan proposal:
- “conceptual” docs page stays stable
- “release notes / compatibility” page is versioned per Private release
- and we add a small checklist in the release process: “Docs PR merged” before GA

Elliot Price: love it. We can also make docs reference the installer’s `bundle inspect` output as the source of truth (“If output differs from this page, trust the tool and open a ticket”).

Sahana Iyer: also: please mention the bundle is a tar.zst and that some unzip tools corrupt files / normalize line endings. we saw that in pilot; it’s a real cause of hash mismatch.

Kira Thompson: yep, I’ll add a “Transport integrity” callout:
- prefer `sha256sum` of the bundle archive before/after transfer
- avoid re-tarring / re-zipping
- use `tar --zstd -tf` to list rather than extracting if you’re just checking

Ethan Park: one more nuance: preflight uses the host clock. In air-gapped envs with time skew, you can hit NOT_YET_VALID. We added a diagnostic override flag for support, but we don’t want it front-and-center.

Dev Patel: can we document that as “for support use only” and point to break-glass / override process?

Elliot Price: yes. we’ll put it under Troubleshooting with big warnings + “contact Support” language.

Kira Thompson: action items I’m taking:
- Draft guide using CLI blocks (no screenshots)
- Add error table + remediation
- Add audit log paths + notes
- Add “transport integrity” and “time skew” callouts

Elliot Price: Ethan/Dylan: please confirm final flag names + exact strings for error messages once you cut RC.

Ethan Park: will do. I’ll drop a final “golden output” once we tag the RC build.

Connor O'Brien: and I’ll add the release checklist item for docs sync. :thumbsup:

Elliot Price: thanks all. keeping this thread open for anything else that should land in the docs PR.

source.body:
Elliot Price: quick q for the status page transparency work — docs is updating the public “how to read incidents” section + post-incident summary format. What can we say publicly when the incident is Dedicated or Private? We keep getting stuck on wording.

Wei Chen: +1. Specifically: do we ever say “Dedicated customers impacted” on the public status page, or do we always keep it to Hosted API components only?

Kira Thompson: we should split Dedicated vs Private. Dedicated is tricky bc there’s shared control plane signals + customer-isolated data plane. Private (VPC/on-prem) is customer-managed env; generally shouldn’t show up on public status page unless it’s our control plane / update service / license service affecting multiple customers.

Elliot Price: ok. so: Dedicated data plane incident for a single customer: no public status update?

Dev Patel: from Support POV: please no. customers hate seeing “everything is fine” on the public page when they’re down, but also we can’t publicly confirm “Customer X dedicated cluster is having capacity issues.” For single-customer Dedicated issues, we handle via ticket + direct comms.

Wei Chen: can we at least say “Some Dedicated customers may be impacted” when we’re not sure if it’s one vs many?

Sean Gallagher: careful. guidance we’ve been pushing in SRE is:
- Public status page is for shared surfaces + broadly scoped impact.
- If Dedicated incident is limited to one tenant/cluster, treat as customer-specific and do not post publicly.
- If impact crosses multiple Dedicated tenants due to shared control plane, shared image rollout, shared auth, shared routing, etc — then yes, we can post a Dedicated component incident.

Kira Thompson: + the copy should avoid implying SLA commitment. Don’t say “within 15 minutes we’ll update”, say “we’ll share updates as we learn more” etc.

Wei Chen: makes sense. what about Dedicated but only in one region? (e.g. “Dedicated - us-east”)

Marissa Cole: product/design direction is: public components can include region where it’s meaningful and non-sensitive. For Dedicated we’ll likely keep it “Dedicated Control Plane” and (maybe) “Dedicated Provisioning” — not per-customer clusters. If there’s a region-wide shared service issue (e.g. provisioning API in us-east), we can say region.

Elliot Price: can we name the region for Private?

Naomi Feldman: only if it’s our shared service / Redwood-managed plane and the region is a Redwood region (e.g. “us-east”). Do NOT disclose customer VPC details, cloud account details, or “air-gapped on-prem customer” etc. Also avoid implying we can see inside customer networks.

Michael Grant: legal note: avoid any statement that could be interpreted as confirming a particular customer’s outage (“Customer environments are down”). Stick to “our control plane service is degraded” / “some customers may experience issues” phrasing. Also avoid “data loss” unless confirmed and approved.

Wei Chen: ok, so what can we safely say for Private if it’s NOT shared? Like customer’s on-prem deployment has an issue.

Kira Thompson: generally: don’t post it publicly. Docs can say: “Private deployments may not be reflected on the public status page. For Private incidents, we will communicate directly through your agreed support channels.”

Dev Patel: +1 please include that sentence. It sets expectation and helps deflect tickets.

Elliot Price: for Dedicated: similar line? “Dedicated incidents may be reported on the status page when they involve shared Redwood-managed components”?

Sean Gallagher: yup. we also need to keep it consistent with the new incident comms templates. We should avoid “we are experiencing X” if it’s limited scope; say “Some customers may experience…” until scope is confirmed.

Wei Chen: can we draft some approved boilerplate for each?

Elliot Price: proposing doc snippets (tell me if ok):
1) Dedicated:
- “Dedicated capacity incidents are reflected on the status page when they affect shared Redwood-managed services (e.g., control plane, provisioning, authentication) or multiple Dedicated tenants. Issues isolated to a single Dedicated deployment are communicated directly to the affected customer(s).”
2) Private:
- “Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments may not be represented on the public status page. When a Private incident is isolated to a specific customer environment, Redwood will communicate directly through your support contact and agreed channels. If a shared Redwood-managed service impacts multiple Private deployments, we may post an update on the status page.”

Naomi Feldman: security-approved in principle. Just avoid listing examples that suggest visibility into customer infra. Your examples are fine since they’re our services.

Michael Grant: legal tweak: “may not be represented” is good. Also recommend “we may post an update” instead of “we will”. No promises.

Kira Thompson: also add: “We won’t share customer-identifying details.” Simple and explicit.

Wei Chen: + for status updates themselves, suggested language blocks?

Sean Gallagher: for Dedicated/Private shared-service incidents:
- Investigating: “We’re investigating reports of elevated errors affecting [Dedicated Control Plane] for some customers.”
- Identified: “We’ve identified an issue with [service class] and are working on mitigation.”
- Mitigating: “A mitigation has been applied and we’re monitoring recovery.”
Avoid: “capacity shortfall”, “tenant”, “noisy neighbor”, specific customer counts.

Dev Patel: and avoid “all Dedicated customers impacted” unless we have hard evidence.

Elliot Price: great. I’ll incorporate this into docs + link to the comms template page. Will also add a callout box: “If you’re seeing an issue not reflected on the status page…” with guidance to contact support.

Wei Chen: can we get a final thumbs up from Kira + Sean on that paragraph before we merge the docs PR?

Kira Thompson: yes, send me the diff.

Sean Gallagher: +1, drop it in #eng-sre too so Comms Leads see same wording.

Elliot Price: will do. thanks all. :thumbsup:

Michael Grant: (last note) if you add post-incident summary guidance, include a line like “We may not include customer-specific details” and avoid listing exact affected customer names/log IDs.

Elliot Price: ack. adding.

source.thread:
Iris Wu: I opened a tiny PR to deprecate the old 'burst_policy' field and add a migration snippet for autoscale -> scale_policy. Also included a short CLI example and an SLA table correction. PR: https://github.com/redwood/docs/pull/5120 Would appreciate quick eyes on wording and the CLI flags.

Ben Carter: Saw it. One big ask: explicitly call out that burst_policy -> scale_policy mapping is lossy for preemption behavior. Add a warning note in the migration section.

Alma Ruiz: Agree with Ben. Also the SLA table row for "dedicated: burst fallback" currently lists an SLA value. We should mark that as "no SLA" and add the footnote about eviction windows.

Noah Green: CLI snippet wording nit: use `redwoodctl capacity create --scale-policy throughput --min 2 --max 8 --cooldown 300 --label tenant=team-zeta` as the example. Keeps flags consistent with the SDK names.

Jules Park: Add a short example showing how to convert old YAML key `burst_policy: {max: 10}` -> `scale_policy: {max_replicas: 10, policy: "burst_compatible"}` and a line saying customers should validate in staging.

Iris Wu: Good catches. I'll add Ben's warning, change the SLA cell to "no SLA (preemptible)", swap CLI to Noah's flags, and include Jules' conversion example.

Ben Carter: One more micro: recommend default cooldown 300s in the migration suggestion, note why (reduced flapping).

Alma Ruiz: After those edits please ping Legal on the SLA footnote wording. We need their ok for the exact phrasing.

docs-bot: Lint: docs/lint passed. Link-check: flagged a broken handbook anchor -> suggested replacement `#capacity-modes`.

Iris Wu: Will update anchor and push a small commit. I'll tag Legal once the wording is in. If CI is green and Legal OK, I'll merge.

Noah Green: lgtm

Jules Park: pushed the conversion example as a tiny commit.

Iris Wu: Thank you all

source.messages:
Amy Walker: Quick heads-up: community reported broken notebook snippets after v2 docs sync. Need urgent patch. Details: examples/notebooks/chat_example.ipynb failing to run due to 'Responses.create' change.
Ben Carter: I'll take notebook fixes. Can someone confirm runtime token var name?
Jill Moreno: v2 uses RW_TOKEN. Notebook still references RW_API_KEY. I can PR the change and add a small note.
Pedro Ruiz: Also saw issues with model alias mapping in sample apps (legacy -> redwood-chat). Opening issue: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/issues/789
Amy Walker: Thx. Also legal wants 'sunset' language eyes — @Legal please confirm.
Legal: Confirmed, use "Deprecated endpoints will be removed after 2025-06-01; calls will receive HTTP 410 with guidance to /docs/migration" in the public notice.
Ben Carter: PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/624 — includes notebook update + test cell that runs a sample call. Added 'try/except' to fail gracefully.
Samira Khan: I'll add a small compatibility shim in examples/compat/shim.js to translate v1 streaming NDJSON to v2 framed events. Snippet:
```js
// simple shim outline
const http = require('http')
http.createServer((req, res) => {
  // proxy to v2, reframe events into legacy ndjson
})
```
Evan Li: Can we add a CI job that runs notebooks (nbconvert execute) and smoke tests against the staging docs? I'll add to .github/workflows/docs-smoke.yml
Amy Walker: Yes please. Make job conditional on label 'deprecation-hotfix' so it runs for these PRs. Docs-Bot: FYI — please add label 'deprecation' and attach smoke output to PR. :robot_face:
Jill Moreno: I'll also add a short user-facing FAQ entry: "Why did my API key header stop working?" with example curl diff.
Pedro Ruiz: Also, community Slack had a config question: how to migrate embeddings to new dim. I added a small script (pad/trim) and linked it in FAQ.
Evan Li: Drafted workflow: run nb tests -> run smoke curl tests -> run snippet-lint -> post artifact. If all pass, merge and deploy.
Amy Walker: Schedule: hotfix PR today, smoke run tonight, merge tomorrow morning. If any failure, hold announcement.
Docs-Bot: Meeting reminder: #docs-hotfix today 16:00 PT.
Ben Carter: Pushed fix. cc @Community-Team to prepare customer comms.
Community-Team: We'll prepare short notice for affected users and include migration snippet and rollback instructions.
Pedro Ruiz: Note: add redirect for /docs/notebooks/chat_example -> /docs/migration/notebooks/chat_example_v2 and include anchor map.
Amy Walker: Great. Assigning owners: notebooks=Ben, snippets=Jill, shim=Samira, CI=Evan, redirects=Pedro. Thanks team. :tada:

source.content:
Kira Thompson: Dropping review feedback for the Private KMS operator guide draft (PR #1288). Overall solid, but a few gaps before we publish.

Kira Thompson: Biggest issue: prerequisites section is too light. We jump right into "set KMS_KEY_ARN" without telling the operator what needs to be true in the env.

Kira Thompson: Missing prereqs I'd like added (bullets):
- Confirm Private control plane can reach cloud KMS endpoints (egress/NAT/proxy). Call out that deny-by-default egress will break decrypt.
- Required IAM principal(s) + where they live (runtime decrypt role vs rotation/admin role). Right now it reads like one role does everything.
- Regional constraints: key must be in same region as KMS calls (and what "region" means for Private).
- Clock sync note (NTP) since some customers have weird TLS/time skew issues.
- "Supported providers" should be explicit (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) + what we mean by "HSM-backed".

Elliot Price: +1. Also the intro should state responsibility boundaries: Redwood encrypts/decrypts via customer KMS, customer owns key lifecycle + access policy; Redwood owns wrapper behavior + telemetry.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agree on responsibility boundaries. We keep getting SE questions where customers assume we store key material.

Bruno Silva: For prereqs: we can add concrete checks from the installer preflight work. We now have a command that validates key + perms.

Bruno Silva: Suggested snippet (once merged):
`redwood-private preflight kms --provider aws --key-arn ... --region ... --principal-arn ...`
It outputs a JSON report + actionable errors.

Kira Thompson: Yes please. Right now troubleshooting says "check permissions" but no steps.

Kira Thompson: Troubleshooting section needs more "if you see X, do Y". Can we add 4-5 common failure examples? The ones I see in support:
1) AccessDenied on Decrypt
2) Throttling / Rate exceeded (causing elevated 5xx)
3) Timeout to KMS endpoint (network/egress)
4) InvalidCiphertextException during/after rotation
5) Key disabled / scheduled deletion

Sean Gallagher: From on-call: we should include log fields + dashboard pointers, otherwise operators don’t know what to send us.

Sean Gallagher: Example fields that show up post-wrapper:
- `kms.error_category` (AccessDenied|Throttling|Timeout|NotFound|InvalidCiphertext)
- `kms.key_arn`
- `request_id` + `trace_id`
And the metric names we page on.

Allison Grant: +1, but careful w/ internal dashboard links in customer-facing docs. Better: "look for metrics prefixed `redwood.kms.*`" and "collect last 15m of metrics + a sample trace".

Hanae Suzuki: On prereqs: please add a callout that policies must allow `Decrypt` *and* `DescribeKey` (AWS) / equivalent metadata read. We’ve seen setups that allow decrypt but not describe -> wrapper errors look like NotFound.

Kira Thompson: Good catch. Also: the guide currently shows a sample AWS policy but it’s too broad (`kms:*` on `*`). Can we swap to least privilege or at least link to our baseline module?

Bruno Silva: I can update the snippet to match the terraform module changes (separate role for runtime decrypt vs rotation admin). Will coordinate with Jordan.

Elliot Price: Another doc edit request: the "Validation" section should come before "Rollout". Operators want to validate KMS works before enabling encryption.

Kira Thompson: Also wording: we say "set KMS timeout to 30s" but no rationale. SRE probably wants a shorter default with retry budget guidance.

Sean Gallagher: Yeah, please don’t recommend 30s as a default. We’re trying to cap tail latency + avoid retry storms. Better guidance:
- Keep per-call timeout low (ex: 2s-5s)
- Keep total retry budget bounded (ex: <= 10s)
- Prefer fail-fast w/ clear error + circuit breaker over long hangs

Hanae Suzuki: For InvalidCiphertext during rotation, can we add a paragraph explaining dual-read window? Something like: "During cutover, services may attempt decrypt with new key version first, then fall back to previous version for a configured window." That’s the core concept customers ask about.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. For Private specifically we should also say "do not delete/disable previous key version until cutover verification is complete".

Kira Thompson: Action items I’ll track for PR #1288:
- [Docs] Add explicit prerequisites section (network reachability, provider support, roles separation, region, time sync)
- [Docs] Add preflight validation command + sample output
- [Docs] Expand troubleshooting matrix w/ 5 concrete examples + remediation steps
- [Eng/Infra] Update IAM policy examples to least privilege + reference terraform module
- [Docs] Add "signals to collect" section (log fields, metrics prefix) without internal links
- [Docs] Add short rotation/dual-read explanation + safety warnings

Docs Bot: PR #1288 status update: 3 reviewers requested changes. Last commit: "Add initial troubleshooting section".

Bruno Silva: I’ll post updated preflight output once PR #392 merges. For now, we can include a placeholder callout "available in installer v1.18+".

Elliot Price: Kira can you ping me when you reorder sections? I’ll do a pass for flow + remove internal jargon.

Kira Thompson: Yep. I’ll push doc edits today and tag @Hanae @Sean for technical accuracy on the troubleshooting examples.

Sean Gallagher: Drop one realistic log line example too (redacted). Helps a lot when customers paste stuff.

Kira Thompson: Will do. I’ll include a fenced block with a sample error + the key fields we need.

Allison Grant: One more nit: please make sure we consistently call it "customer-managed KMS" in the Private context. Avoid "bring your own keys" since it gets conflated w/ BYOK for SaaS.

Kira Thompson: Noted. Updating terminology to "customer-managed key" / "customer-managed KMS" throughout.

Vivek Kulkarni: Thanks all. This will cut down SE back-and-forth for regulated prospects.

source.messages:
Kira Thompson: Heads up: opened docs PR for the Regulated Workloads landing page + cross-links. Would love eyes today/tomorrow so we can ship before the webinar promo. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-docs/pull/421

Kira Thompson: Review asks:
- PMM: headline/value prop + “approved language” check
- Security: claims + links to Security FAQ / evidence pack index
- Docs: final checklist (nav, redirects, SEO, broken links)

Elliot Price: :eyes: taking a first pass now. Kira can you drop the intended final URL path + where it sits in nav?

Kira Thompson: Planned path: `/solutions/regulated-workloads/` (matches other solution pages). In nav: Solutions > Regulated workloads (new). Also added a footer link from Security page.

GitHub: @redwood-docs-bot CI checks started for PR #421 (build, linkcheck, lint).

Soojin Lee: Will review copy. Quick note up front: please avoid “HIPAA-ready” / “HIPAA compliant” language anywhere. We should stick to “supports PHI-adjacent workloads” / “deployment controls that help meet internal compliance requirements” etc.

Kira Thompson: Yep, I used “PHI-adjacent” and “regulated workloads” only. No HIPAA or PCI claims.

Naomi Feldman: Looping in. I’m mainly looking for:
1) No over-claims about SOC2/ISO (must be precise)
2) KMS/HSM phrasing (don’t imply we run inside customer HSM)
3) Evidence pack access control (should be “available under NDA/request”)

Ben Carter: Thanks. For CTA we want: primary = “Request a security review” (routes to form), secondary = “Talk to an engineer” or “Get an architecture review”. Can we keep “Download evidence pack” off the public page?

Elliot Price: +1 to not offering a direct evidence pack download. We can link to a request page or a short “what’s included” page.

Daniel Carter: Evidence pack index exists (internal) and we have a sanitized “index” we can share, but it still shouldn’t be publicly discoverable. Recommend copy: “Evidence pack available upon request” with a contact CTA.

Kira Thompson: Current PR has:
- “Security FAQ for regulated workloads” link (public docs page)
- “Evidence pack (available upon request)” link -> takes you to a short page that explains contents and points to request form
No direct SOC2 link.

Naomi Feldman: Good. Where does “Security FAQ” land? If it’s the sales-ready Confluence doc, we can’t link that publicly.

Kira Thompson: Not Confluence. I created a docs page under `/security/regulated-workloads-faq/` with the approved Q&A (sanitized) + “contact security” at the bottom. It intentionally doesn’t include report artifacts.

Soojin Lee: Reading now. One nit: section header “Compliance & Certifications” reads like we’re listing a bunch. Suggest “Security posture” and keep it to a few bullets + “details available under NDA.”

Kira Thompson: Makes sense. I’ll rename header and tighten.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Skimmed the page. Overall direction is fine. A few wording tweaks:
- Replace “SOC 2 certified” with “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” (we never say “certified”)
- “Data residency” should be “deployment region and data locality controls” unless we’re explicitly committing to residency guarantees
- For encryption: “in transit and at rest” is ok, but don’t say “end-to-end”

Kira Thompson: Got it. Will update those exact phrases.

Elliot Price: Docs checklist items I’m watching:
- Add to sitemap + set canonical URL
- Meta title/description (SEO)
- No orphaned pages; ensure nav and internal links are consistent
- If we’re adding `/solutions/regulated-workloads/`, we should add redirect from any prior draft path (if it existed)

Kira Thompson: Added canonical + meta, but will double-check. There was a draft at `/solutions/regulated/` in a branch a while ago (never shipped). No redirect in prod.

Tyler Benson: For the CTA form links, please add UTMs consistent w/ the ABM pilot:
`utm_source=docs&utm_medium=owned&utm_campaign=abm_regulated&utm_content=regulated_landing`
Also for the “Request a security review” button, can we tag `utm_term=security_review`?

Kira Thompson: Yep, I’ll add UTMs across both CTAs.

Grace O'Connor: Quick SDR workflow question: where do inbound requests land? We need them to hit the “Regulated ABM” routing rule in HubSpot (so we don’t lose these).

Ben Carter: Good call. Tyler + I have a routing rule already for `abm_regulated` campaign. As long as UTMs are set, we’re good.

Naomi Feldman: One more: on the FAQ page, in the “Audit logs” answer, please avoid listing specific field names unless we’re 100% sure they won’t change. Better: “request/response metadata, access events, admin actions” and then “sample schema available in evidence pack.”

Kira Thompson: I did list a few fields (request_id, model, timestamp). I’ll generalize and point to sample schema via request.

Soojin Lee: Copy suggestion: replace “predictable throughput” with “predictable performance under load (reserved capacity options)” in the hero. Makes it less jargon-y.

Kira Thompson: Updated.

GitHub: @redwood-docs-bot CI checks passed for PR #421 (build ok, lint ok, linkcheck ok).

Elliot Price: I left inline comments on: nav label capitalization, two broken anchors (minor), and the canonical tag. Otherwise looks ready once Security edits are applied.

Kira Thompson: Pushed fixes for anchors + header renames + SOC2 wording + audit log answer. PTAL.

Naomi Feldman: Reviewed latest commit. Looks good now. Approved from Security side as long as we keep the evidence pack link as “request access” (not a public download). :thumbsup:

Camila Reyes: Great. Ben/Kira let’s ship today if reviews are green. Also: can you add a tiny note under CTA: “We can share compliance documentation under NDA” (keeps expectations set).

Kira Thompson: Added the note (short + non-committal) and requested final approval from Elliot + Soojin.

Soojin Lee: Approved. Thanks for incorporating the language guardrails.

Elliot Price: Approved on my side. Merge when ready.

Kira Thompson: Merging now + will monitor deploy. Thanks all.

Kira Thompson: I did list a few fields (request_id, model, timestamp). I’ll generalize and point to sample schema via request.

Soojin Lee: Copy suggestion: replace “predictable throughput” with “predictable performance under load (reserved capacity options)” in the hero. Makes it less jargon-y.

Kira Thompson: Updated.

GitHub: @redwood-docs-bot CI checks passed for PR #421 (build ok, lint ok, linkcheck ok).

Elliot Price: I left inline comments on: nav label capitalization, two broken anchors (minor), and the canonical tag. Otherwise looks ready once Security edits are applied.

Kira Thompson: Pushed fixes for anchors + header renames + SOC2 wording + audit log answer. PTAL.

Naomi Feldman: Reviewed latest commit. Looks good now. Approved from Security side as long as we keep the evidence pack link as “request access” (not a public download). :thumbsup:

Camila Reyes: Great. Ben/Kira let’s ship today if reviews are green. Also: can you add a tiny note under CTA: “We can share compliance documentation under NDA” (keeps expectations set).

Kira Thompson: Added the note (short + non-committal) and requested final approval from Elliot + Soojin.

Soojin Lee: Approved. Thanks for incorporating the language guardrails.

Elliot Price: Approved on my side. Merge when ready.

Kira Thompson: Merging now + will monitor deploy. Thanks all.

source.messages:
Jordan Blake: Heads-up — production link-check failed after the midday content sync. Error pointing to a missing anchor `#cli-integration` from /docs/quickstart.html.
Jordan Blake: Log snippet: ```Error: Could not find anchor #cli-integration referenced from /docs/quickstart.html: line 78```
Eva Lopez: That page had a heading `## CLI Integration (macOS, Linux)` which I recently changed to `## CLI Integration (macOS / Linux) 🛠️` to be more friendly. Looks like the emoji and slash changed the slug.
Chris Meyer: emoji in headings is the usual culprit. Docusaurus slugify behaves differently across versions and will %-encode emoji or change the slug.
Jordan Blake: Options: revert heading, add explicit MDX id `{#cli-integration}`, or update all incoming links to the new encoded slug. Reverting loses the UX change.
Eva Lopez: I prefer adding explicit ids so we can keep the emoji. I'll open a quick PR to add `{#cli-integration}` to the heading and add a small docs CI check for link stability.
Chris Meyer: while you're at it, grep for other emoji in headings: `rg "^## .*\p{Emoji}" -n docs || true` — we had a couple previously.
Eva Lopez: Found 4 headings with emoji: quickstart, release-notes, and two guides. I'll add ids for all of them and update CONTRIBUTING with guidance: "avoid inline emoji in headings unless adding an explicit id."
Eva Lopez: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/1033 — adds explicit heading ids, CONTRIBUTING blurb, and a new CI job that runs `npx markdown-link-check -c .mlc-config.json site/**/*.html` after build.
docs-bot: label added: docs-review. reviewers requested: Jordan Blake, Chris Meyer.
Jordan Blake: LGTM minor nit: remove trailing spaces on the id lines.
Chris Meyer: Also add a short unit script `scripts/ci/check-heading-ids.sh` that fails if any heading contains emoji or NBSP without an explicit `{#...}`. Helps catch new ones.
Eva Lopez: Added script and fixed whitespace. Squashed and pushed.
ci-bot: Running docs CI for PR 1033...
ci-bot: \u2714 build succeeded, \u2714 markdown link-check passed.
docs-bot: PR #1033 merged by Eva Lopez.
ci-bot: :white_check_mark: deploy/docs-staging succeeded (build 425).
Jordan Blake: Verified on staging: anchor resolves and quickstart links work. Keeping emoji — nice touch.
Chris Meyer: Filed follow-up issue to add pre-commit hook suggestion: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/issues/226 — "lint: warn on emoji in headings unless id present."
Eva Lopez: I'll add a short example in the contribution guide showing how to add `{#id}` for headings that need non-alphanumeric characters.
Jordan Blake: Great. Closing thread once follow-up issue is triaged.
