source.messages:
Elliot Price: Heads up: doing final review pass on retention controls docs + internal Security FAQ today. Need sign-off on wording before we tell SEs to start using it in questionnaires. Links:
- Docs PR: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/1832
- FAQ draft (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood/security-faq/retention-controls-and-compliance-reporting
Main questions:
1) How we describe “prompts/completions where stored” (avoid implying we store by default)
2) Purge semantics language (avoid “immediate deletion” promises)
3) Legal hold / incident hold wording (who can approve + what it means)

Kira Thompson: I read PR-1832. Overall good. Biggest nit: page currently says “we retain request/response logs for 30 days by default.” That’s not true for every tier and can be configured down. Suggest: “Default retention varies by deployment tier and can be configured by an admin; see Retention Defaults table.” Also the “where stored” parenthetical is doing a lot of work — might need a clearer statement.

Naomi Feldman: +1. For questionnaires we keep getting “Do you store customer prompts?” and “Can customers opt out of logging?” We should explicitly say: Redwood does not persist prompts/completions unless logging features are enabled by the customer (and/or required for a configured feature). And even then, retention is governed by the tenant retention policy.

Marcus Lin: Agree but careful: we do have transient processing + streaming, and some telemetry can include snippets if customers enable it. We should avoid absolute “never” language. Suggest phrasing:
- “Prompts/completions are processed in-memory for inference.”
- “Persisted storage is optional and controlled via logging/telemetry settings; when enabled, retention applies.”
Also: please remove “hard delete” unless we define it. Use “scheduled deletion” / “purge job”.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll replace “we store” with “may be stored if enabled” and link to “Data classes” section. On the purge semantics: current text says “purged daily.” Is that ok or should we state a window/SLO?

Daniel Carter: For audit evidence we’ve been saying “deletion runs on a schedule; deletions are observable and verifiable via metrics.” If we give an SLO, it needs to match what SRE will support. Maybe: “typically within 24 hours” but not contractual.

Rachel Kim: From CS angle: questionnaires often ask “How fast do you delete after retention expires?” If we don’t say anything, it becomes a back-and-forth. Could we say “within 24h target” with caveat “best-effort unless otherwise contracted”?

Marcus Lin: Let’s not publish “24h” broadly as a promise. We can say “on a scheduled basis (at least daily) and monitored.” Internally we can maintain the SLO in runbooks, but docs should be conservative.

Olga Petrov: +1 conservative. For SOC/ISO style control language, we should emphasize: defined retention periods, automated enforcement, monitoring/alerts, and evidence export. Don’t add a numeric SLO unless Legal okays.

Markus Klein: SE feedback: the FAQ needs a crisp answer to “Can you support 7-day retention?” and “Can you support 0-day retention?” Current FAQ says “configurable” but doesn’t set bounds. For sales we need: “minimum retention depends on data class and tier” and point to “contact us for Private” type language.

Naomi Feldman: The schema has min TTLs per tier/data class. But we shouldn’t publish the exact mins in public docs yet; it changes. For FAQ, we can say:
- “Retention can be reduced subject to minimums required for platform reliability and auditability.”
- “Customers can also disable certain logging entirely (where supported).”
And then: “Ask your SE for current supported ranges.”

Kira Thompson: I can update the docs to say “Customers can disable request/response logging” but we need to be explicit that audit logs can’t be fully disabled (at least on Hosted/Dedicated). Is that correct?

Marcus Lin: Correct. Audit logs are part of the security control plane; we retain them per policy and they’re required for security monitoring. Customers can configure retention, not disable entirely (Hosted/Dedicated). For Private, depends on deployment, but “auditability required” still stands.

Elliot Price: I’ll add:
- “Audit logs are generated for administrative and security-relevant actions; they cannot be fully disabled in Hosted/Dedicated.”
- “Retention can be configured by authorized admins.”
Any wording preference: “administrative actions” vs “control plane actions”?

Olga Petrov: “Administrative and security-relevant actions” reads better for auditors.

Connor O'Brien: Release note concern: if docs mention “Retention Settings page in Console” we need to ensure feature flag status. Are we ok to say “available to Dedicated/Private design partners” vs “generally available”?

Naomi Feldman: Please gate it. Say “Console supports viewing/editing retention settings (availability varies by plan and rollout stage).” And for now include “Contact support” pathway.

Kira Thompson: On legal hold: PR currently says “we support legal holds.” Might imply self-serve. Should say “supported via support process” and “requires approval.” Who’s the approver in wording?

Marcus Lin: For external: “Legal hold can be applied to eligible data sets upon written request through support, subject to authorization and validation.” Don’t name internal approvers. Internally it’s Security/Legal.

Daniel Carter: For internal FAQ we can keep the approval matrix reference (GRC + Security + Legal). For customer-facing docs, keep it generic.

Rachel Kim: Also add an FAQ line: “Does a legal hold override configured retention?” Answer: “Yes, for the data under hold; deletion is suspended until hold is lifted; actions are audited.” This comes up a lot.

Elliot Price: Noted.

Markus Klein: One more: questionnaires ask “Do you provide a retention configuration export?” We should explicitly say the compliance report pack includes it, and that audit log exports can be time-windowed.

Olga Petrov: Yes. Please ensure the compliance pack section lists:
- audit-log coverage summary
- retention configuration export
- data residency/region summary
- RBAC/access control report
- evidence checklist
And clarify “baseline pack” vs “custom requests” so we don’t commit to bespoke.

Kira Thompson: I’ll tweak the compliance pack page to match that list and add: “Exports provided in machine-readable formats; packaged for customer security reviews.” (No mention of PDF yet.)

Elliot Price: Great. Last-minute phrasing changes I’m planning in PR:
- Replace “hard delete” with “scheduled purge (automated deletion jobs)”
- Replace “purged daily” with “purged on a scheduled basis; timing depends on workload and store; monitored for backlog”
- Replace “we store prompts” with “prompts/completions may be stored only if logging/telemetry features are enabled; retention applies”
- Add “audit logs cannot be fully disabled (Hosted/Dedicated)”
Please react with :+1: or comment if objections.

Naomi Feldman: :+1: (please also add the questionnaire-friendly Q/A snippet to the internal FAQ: “Do you store prompts?” “Is retention configurable?” “Can you export audit logs?”)

Marcus Lin: :+1: with one edit: say “processed in-memory for inference” and “not used for training” (if we’re comfortable). We usually get that question bundled.

Elliot Price: We can add “Redwood does not use customer inputs/outputs to train models” in FAQ. (That’s standard.) I’ll keep it out of the retention doc itself.

Olga Petrov: :+1: Also please change “compliance report” to “compliance reporting pack” consistently. Auditors read “report” as an attestation.

Rachel Kim: :+1: And add “If you shorten retention aggressively, you may reduce troubleshooting visibility; break-glass exception via support” (word carefully).

Marcus Lin: Careful on “break-glass” externally. Better: “In limited cases, support may request temporary adjustments for incident response, with approval and audit trail.”

Kira Thompson: I can incorporate that as “Incident response exception process (by request)” in the FAQ only.

Elliot Price: Done. I’ll push updates to PR-1832 in ~30 mins and re-request review from Naomi + Marcus + Olga. Goal is merge EOD.

Connor O'Brien: Once merged, ping me and I’ll add it to the enablement bundle for SEs (so we don’t circulate stale links).

Elliot Price: Will do. Thanks all.

Naomi Feldman: Please gate it. Say “Console supports viewing/editing retention settings (availability varies by plan and rollout stage).” And for now include “Contact support” pathway.

Kira Thompson: On legal hold: PR currently says “we support legal holds.” Might imply self-serve. Should say “supported via support process” and “requires approval.” Who’s the approver in wording?

Marcus Lin: For external: “Legal hold can be applied to eligible data sets upon written request through support, subject to authorization and validation.” Don’t name internal approvers. Internally it’s Security/Legal.

Daniel Carter: For internal FAQ we can keep the approval matrix reference (GRC + Security + Legal). For customer-facing docs, keep it generic.

Rachel Kim: Also add an FAQ line: “Does a legal hold override configured retention?” Answer: “Yes, for the data under hold; deletion is suspended until hold is lifted; actions are audited.” This comes up a lot.

Elliot Price: Noted.

Markus Klein: One more: questionnaires ask “Do you provide a retention configuration export?” We should explicitly say the compliance report pack includes it, and that audit log exports can be time-windowed.

Olga Petrov: Yes. Please ensure the compliance pack section lists:
- audit-log coverage summary
- retention configuration export
- data residency/region summary
- RBAC/access control report
- evidence checklist
And clarify “baseline pack” vs “custom requests” so we don’t commit to bespoke.

Kira Thompson: I’ll tweak the compliance pack page to match that list and add: “Exports provided in machine-readable formats; packaged for customer security reviews.” (No mention of PDF yet.)

Elliot Price: Great. Last-minute phrasing changes I’m planning in PR:
- Replace “hard delete” with “scheduled purge (automated deletion jobs)”
- Replace “purged daily” with “purged on a scheduled basis; timing depends on workload and store; monitored for backlog”
- Replace “we store prompts” with “prompts/completions may be stored only if logging/telemetry features are enabled; retention applies”
- Add “audit logs cannot be fully disabled (Hosted/Dedicated)”
Please react with :+1: or comment if objections.

Naomi Feldman: :+1: (please also add the questionnaire-friendly Q/A snippet to the internal FAQ: “Do you store prompts?” “Is retention configurable?” “Can you export audit logs?”)

Marcus Lin: :+1: with one edit: say “processed in-memory for inference” and “not used for training” (if we’re comfortable). We usually get that question bundled.

Elliot Price: We can add “Redwood does not use customer inputs/outputs to train models” in FAQ. (That’s standard.) I’ll keep it out of the retention doc itself.

Olga Petrov: :+1: Also please change “compliance report” to “compliance reporting pack” consistently. Auditors read “report” as an attestation.

Rachel Kim: :+1: And add “If you shorten retention aggressively, you may reduce troubleshooting visibility; break-glass exception via support” (word carefully).

Marcus Lin: Careful on “break-glass” externally. Better: “In limited cases, support may request temporary adjustments for incident response, with approval and audit trail.”

Kira Thompson: I can incorporate that as “Incident response exception process (by request)” in the FAQ only.

Elliot Price: Done. I’ll push updates to PR-1832 in ~30 mins and re-request review from Naomi + Marcus + Olga. Goal is merge EOD.

Connor O'Brien: Once merged, ping me and I’ll add it to the enablement bundle for SEs (so we don’t circulate stale links).

Elliot Price: Will do. Thanks all.

source.messages:
Kira Thompson: need to lock the customer-facing FAQ for Private backup/restore today so we can merge docs PR. can folks review these 3 areas: (1) what’s backed up, (2) how customers should test restores, (3) support boundaries

Kira Thompson: draft FAQ section here (wip): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/812#discussion_r_FAQ

Elliot Price: skimmed. overall good. a couple questions read too “internal runbook-y”. we should keep it crisp + avoid implying we back up the entire k8s cluster by default.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. biggest customer confusion: “is this like velero?” they expect full cluster snapshots. we need to clearly say we back up Redwood control plane state + required config, not arbitrary namespaces.

Sean Gallagher: yep. and please add a line that restore testing should happen in a non-prod environment first. we’ve had folks try to “test” by restoring over prod and then open a P0 :)

Marcus Lin: from security side: make sure we’re explicit that we do NOT include plaintext secret material in the backup artifact (only references), unless customer explicitly configures a secret backend export on their side.

Hanae Suzuki: re secrets: we should spell out “KMS key access is required at restore time” (for envelope encryption). otherwise folks in airgapped/locked-down envs get surprised.

Kira Thompson: ok, proposing FAQ items below — pls nit and confirm wording:

Kira Thompson: Q: What does Redwood back up?
A: Redwood Private backup captures control-plane state required to reconstruct tenants/projects, routing/config policies, and platform configuration that drives serving behavior. It also captures the deployment’s release/config values needed for deterministic redeploy.

Kira Thompson: Q: What is NOT included?
A: Data-plane runtime state (pods are treated as stateless), customer application data, and model artifacts stored outside the Redwood control plane. Kubernetes cluster-level state (e.g., full etcd) is not automatically backed up by Redwood.

Kira Thompson: Q: Are secrets included?
A: Backup artifacts are encrypted, but Redwood does not export plaintext secrets by default. Backups include references/metadata to external secret stores (KMS/Secrets Manager/Vault) and require those systems + keys to be available during restore.

Kira Thompson: Q: How do I test restores?
A: Run a periodic restore drill into a staging cluster using the same backup storage backend and key material, then run the validation checklist (health checks + API smoke tests). Do not test restores by overwriting prod.

Kira Thompson: Q: What help can Redwood Support provide?
A: Support can provide guidance, review logs/validation reports, and help interpret errors, but cannot access customer infrastructure or run commands in customer environments. For hands-on assistance, engage Solutions/Professional Services.

Markus Klein: wording looks right. can we add one more in support boundary: “Support will not guarantee RPO/RTO” / “RPO/RTO depends on your backup cadence + infra” (customers keep trying to make it an SLA).

Vivek Kulkarni: yes please. also add a Q about “How often should we run backups / restore drills?” even if answer is “depends” but give recommended starting point.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we can give a safe default: daily backups + weekly/monthly restore drill depending on environment criticality. and link to “restore validation test plan” (customer-safe subset).

Elliot Price: also, for “what does Redwood back up” we should list concrete components in plain language. otherwise people assume everything.

Marcus Lin: on secrets answer: avoid “metadata” (too vague). maybe “references to secrets (names/paths), not secret values”. and explicitly call out that the KMS key must be accessible and permissions valid at restore time.

Hanae Suzuki: also call out key rotation: “older backups remain decryptable if key policy allows”. maybe too deep for FAQ, but at least mention: if you revoke key access, you can’t restore.

Kira Thompson: ok updated phrasing (proposed):

Kira Thompson: Q: What does the Redwood Private backup include?
A: The backup includes Redwood control-plane state and configuration required to rebuild the Private deployment, including:
• tenant/project configuration and routing policy state
• control-plane databases/config stores (Redwood-managed)
• the installer/Helm configuration needed to redeploy the same version (release values/manifests)
Backups are stored as encrypted artifacts in your configured backend (e.g., S3+KMS, NFS).

Kira Thompson: Q: What does it NOT include?
A: The backup does not include:
• data-plane runtime state (GPU workers are treated as stateless)
• customer application data or third-party system data
• plaintext secret values
• a full Kubernetes cluster snapshot (e.g., etcd). If you need cluster-level recovery, use your standard Kubernetes/infra tooling.

Kira Thompson: Q: Are secrets included?
A: No. Backups include references to secrets (names/paths) but not the secret values themselves. Restore requires access to your secret store and the encryption keys used to protect the backup artifact (KMS/HSM where configured).

Kira Thompson: Q: How do we test restores?
A: We recommend scheduled restore drills into a non-production cluster:
1) verify access to the backup backend + keys
2) restore into a clean staging cluster using the same Redwood version
3) run the post-restore validation checklist (health checks + API smoke tests)
Do not test restores by restoring “over” your production control plane.

Kira Thompson: Q: What are Redwood’s recommended backup/DR cadences?
A: As a starting point, run daily backups and perform restore drills at least monthly (more frequently for higher-criticality deployments). Your achievable RPO/RTO depends on backup frequency, storage performance, and your infrastructure automation.

Kira Thompson: Q: What help can Redwood Support provide?
A: Support can help you interpret errors, review logs/validation reports, and provide guidance on documented procedures. Support cannot access your environment or execute commands in customer infrastructure. Hands-on assistance is available via Solutions/Professional Services. RPO/RTO commitments are customer-owned unless explicitly contracted.

Elliot Price: this is much clearer. small nit: “installer/Helm configuration needed to redeploy” — customers may not use Helm directly (some do Terraform). maybe say “deployment configuration (Helm values / installer config)”.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also can we link to a “validation checklist” section in docs rather than “smoke tests” only? but ok for FAQ.

Marcus Lin: secrets answer looks good now. pls add one explicit sentence: “If the decryption key is unavailable (revoked policy / deleted key), restore will fail.” that’s important.

Vivek Kulkarni: support boundary line is perfect. we should keep “unless explicitly contracted” (enterprise asks).

Markus Klein: can we soften “customer-owned” -> “depends on your environment and runbooks” (less confrontational).

Kira Thompson: ack. revised support answer:
“RPO/RTO depends on your environment and operational procedures unless explicitly covered in your contract.”

Kira Thompson: adding Marcus sentence to secrets:
“If the decryption key is unavailable (e.g., access revoked or key deleted), restore will fail.”

Elliot Price: :thumbsup: ship it.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: works for me.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: 

Hanae Suzuki: :thumbsup: (and ty for adding the key availability callout)

Kira Thompson: cool, I’ll update the PR text + ping for final review. thanks all.

Kira Thompson: (also adding a short “Velero/k8s backups” note in a non-FAQ section to avoid the “full cluster snapshot” assumption)

source.messages:
Marissa Cole: hey docs folks + eng — can we get eyes on the Route Experiments customer guide before we merge? PR here: https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/1447

Marissa Cole: specifically want feedback on troubleshooting section + whether our examples are clear enough for beta customers (we got a bunch of “what do these headers mean” last week)

Redwood Docs Bot: PR #1447 build preview ready: https://docs-preview.redwood.ai/pr-1447/route-experiments

Dylan Brooks: :eyes: taking a pass now. first impression: overall solid, but troubleshooting needs a “symptoms -> likely cause -> what to check” table. right now it’s more narrative.

Felix Schneider: +1. also think we should add one screenshot callout in Results page about “data freshness” / aggregation delay. Support ticket SUP-24910 was basically “results not updating” and it was just rollup job lag.

Chloe Martin: skimmed quickly — API bits look ok but we should be precise about stop semantics.

Chloe Martin: docs currently say “Stop immediately removes traffic.” in reality we have draining for some routes (depending on streaming) + control plane propagation lag. can we reword to “Stops new assignments; in-flight requests may complete” + mention typical propagation window?

Rafael Mendes: +1 and please include an explicit “guardrails are soft-stop by default” statement. we keep getting assumptions that it will hard-stop and revert automatically.

Marissa Cole: yep, agreed. we can add “soft-stop by default; hard-stop optional (if enabled by org admin)” with a link to guardrails page.

Jordan Lee: deterministic assignment section: can we be explicit about stickiness key precedence? people keep passing random IDs and then wonder why split looks skewed.

Jordan Lee: current precedence (per ADR-047):
1) explicit `x-redwood-sticky-key` header (or SDK equivalent)
2) customer-provided user id header if configured (some enterprise gateways)
3) fallback generated key (NOT recommended; low entropy risk)

Jordan Lee: also mention: if you don’t provide a stable key, you *should not* expect stable assignment across requests.

Logan Wright: + telemetry side: we should add a troubleshooting bullet for “attribution drop” esp on streaming + retries. we fixed one path (PR 28622), but customers still can see missing `experiment_id/variant_id` if they’re sampling spans aggressively.

Logan Wright: suggestion: include a short snippet for checking response headers and note that metrics attribution is based on server-side context; headers are for debugging only.

Allison Grant: please also add the cardinality note. we intentionally do NOT tag every metric with every dimension. customers ask “why can’t i break down by user_id” — we need to set expectations.

Allison Grant: maybe a callout box:
- Always available: `{route, experiment_id, variant_id, region}`
- Sometimes available / sampled: `{model_id}`
- Not available: arbitrary user attributes

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section: the guide says “anyone with Console access can stop an experiment.” that’s not right.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: minimum roles:
- Viewer: can view results + config
- Operator: can start/stop/ramp traffic
- Admin: can create/update/delete + enable hard-stop policy

Kaitlyn Nguyen: (names might differ in UI but we should at least say “depends on org RBAC” + point to permissions page)

Connor O'Brien: release note-ish: can we add a “Beta limitations” section? for GA checklist we’ve been repeating:
- no statistical significance engine
- no custom metrics ingestion
- results can lag by X minutes

Connor O'Brien: it helps set expectations so CS doesn’t have to explain on every call.

Dylan Brooks: specific copy edits:
- “variant headers” section should show exact header names (case sensitive) + an example response.

Dylan Brooks: right now it says “we include variant headers.” i’d add:
```
# response headers (debugging)
x-redwood-experiment-id: exp_2f8c...
x-redwood-experiment-variant-id: var_b
x-redwood-route-id: route_chat_completions
```

Dylan Brooks: also show how to echo request id so support can find it.

Marissa Cole: ok. can someone paste a minimal curl example we can drop in?

Jordan Lee: here’s a simple one (no auth shown):
```
curl -sS https://api.redwood.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-redwood-sticky-key: user_12345' \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"Write a 2 sentence summary of Redwood Inference."}],
    "stream": false
  }' -D -
```

Jordan Lee: `-D -` is key since it prints headers.

Logan Wright: troubleshooting bullets i’d add verbatim:
- “I see 90/10 configured but my dashboard looks 100/0”: usually missing or low-entropy sticky key -> check request headers; also look for “variant 0 traffic” alert.
- “Console shows no data for variant”: attribution drop or experiment not started in that region.
- “Metrics don’t match my client timing”: Console uses server-side latency (router->completion), client includes network.

Allison Grant: can we include the metric definitions? just a quick one-liner each:
- latency p95/p99 = server-side request duration
- cost/token = billed tokens * unit price (may differ by model)
- error rate = non-2xx + router errors

Rafael Mendes: and for “results stale”:
- check config propagation lag metric
- check rollup job delay
- what to do: wait X min; if > threshold, contact support with experiment_id + region

Chloe Martin: + “stop semantics” snippet:
- Stop prevents *new* assignments.
- Existing sticky users may still route to prior variant until propagation completes.

Chloe Martin: skimmed quickly — API bits look ok but we should be precise about stop semantics.

Chloe Martin: docs currently say “Stop immediately removes traffic.” in reality we have draining for some routes (depending on streaming) + control plane propagation lag. can we reword to “Stops new assignments; in-flight requests may complete” + mention typical propagation window?

Rafael Mendes: +1 and please include an explicit “guardrails are soft-stop by default” statement. we keep getting assumptions that it will hard-stop and revert automatically.

Jordan Lee: current precedence (per ADR-047):

2) customer-provided user id header if configured (some enterprise gateways)

Jordan Lee: also mention: if you don’t provide a stable key, you *should not* expect stable assignment across requests.

Logan Wright: suggestion: include a short snippet for checking response headers and note that metrics attribution is based on server-side context; headers are for debugging only.

Allison Grant: please also add the cardinality note. we intentionally do NOT tag every metric with every dimension. customers ask “why can’t i break down by user_id” — we need to set expectations.

Allison Grant: maybe a callout box:
- Always available: `{route, experiment_id, variant_id, region}`
- Sometimes available / sampled: `{model_id}`
- Not available: arbitrary user attributes

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section: the guide says “anyone with Console access can stop an experiment.” that’s not right.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: minimum roles:
- Viewer: can view results + config
- Operator: can start/stop/ramp traffic
- Admin: can create/update/delete + enable hard-stop policy

Kaitlyn Nguyen: (names might differ in UI but we should at least say “depends on org RBAC” + point to permissions page)

Connor O'Brien: release note-ish: can we add a “Beta limitations” section? for GA checklist we’ve been repeating:
- no statistical significance engine
- no custom metrics ingestion
- results can lag by X minutes

Connor O'Brien: it helps set expectations so CS doesn’t have to explain on every call.

Dylan Brooks: specific copy edits:
- “variant headers” section should show exact header names (case sensitive) + an example response.

Dylan Brooks: right now it says “we include variant headers.” i’d add:
```
# response headers (debugging)
x-redwood-experiment-id: exp_2f8c...
x-redwood-experiment-variant-id: var_b
x-redwood-route-id: route_chat_completions
```

Dylan Brooks: also show how to echo request id so support can find it.

Marissa Cole: ok. can someone paste a minimal curl example we can drop in?

Jordan Lee: here’s a simple one (no auth shown):

curl -sS https://api.redwood.ai/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'x-redwood-sticky-key: user_12345' \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"Write a 2 sentence summary of Redwood Inference."}],
    "stream": false
  }' -D -
```

- error rate = non-2xx + router errors

Redwood Docs Bot: Marissa Cole pushed commits to PR #1447: "Clarify stop semantics + add troubleshooting table"

source.text:
Elliot Price: Kicking off docs coordination for SIEM-ready audit export beta. We have a few artifacts in flight (integration guide, Security FAQ, retention/backfill language). Need ownership + review path so we don’t ship contradictory wording.

Elliot Price: Current drafts/targets:
- Internal CS/SE guide: Splunk HEC setup + troubleshooting
- Security FAQ (customer-facing-ish, via Sales enablement)
- Admin docs for Console: “Audit Export Settings” + “Status/Health” + “Test connection”
- Retention vs export responsibilities (GRC language)

Elliot Price: @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei can you confirm who in Security is primary reviewer for wording around (a) PII minimization/redaction and (b) delivery guarantees (at-least-once, dedup expectations)?

Marcus Lin: I can be primary on semantics + PII boundary. Kevin can do retention/backfill + compliance questionnaire language. Also want to make sure we don’t accidentally promise “exactly-once”.

Kevin Osei: +1. Happy to own the “what we retain vs what customer retains” section and any SOC2/ISO phrasing.

Miguel Santos: From CS side: we need a crisp “what to expect in beta” section + troubleshooting decision tree (Splunk 4xx/5xx, Datadog 403/429, S3 access denied). I can own the troubleshooting content if someone gives me the technical bullets.

Colin O'Donnell: I can provide technical bullets + screenshots for Splunk HEC (token, index, sourcetype) and common errors. Also note Splunk HEC will 429 hard; exporter backoff is non-trivial.

Naomi Feldman: Re: “beta limitations must be explicit”:
- Hosted + Dedicated only (no Private/on-prem in beta)
- Backfill window limited (we’re not doing unlimited historical export)
- Delivery is at-least-once; customer should dedup on `event_id`
- Some fields conditional (IP/UA only for certain event types)

Naomi Feldman: We should align on the exact backfill limit that we want to publicly state (and that matches product defaults).

Dylan Brooks: For Console admin docs: I can get you UI copy (empty states + “Test connection” flow). But please don’t document knobs we might hide behind FF (filter builder advanced predicates). We’re trying to keep v1 simple.

Elliot Price: Great. Proposing ownership:
- Console admin docs (how to configure + status): Elliot (with Dylan for UI copy)
- Splunk HEC internal guide (CS/SE): Colin (with Miguel for troubleshooting structure)
- Security FAQ: Elliot to draft, Security to review/sign off
- Retention vs export responsibilities: Kevin (with Elliot to format/publish)

Elliot Price: Review chain suggestion:
1) Eliot drafts -> 2) Security review (Marcus/Kevin) -> 3) CS review (Miguel) -> 4) PM final (Naomi) for scope/roadmap statements.

Marcus Lin: Works. For Security review, I specifically want:
- No raw request/response payloads in examples (avoid customer prompts, completion text)
- Explicit statement: audit export contains metadata about actions, not model I/O
- PII section: “source_ip/user_agent may appear where applicable” + mention redaction rules for actor identifiers (email hashing? depends on schema)

Elliot Price: On that last point: are we hashing emails/user identifiers in audit events? I thought we were storing actor as user_id + display name (if present) but not full email.

Kevin Osei: Current direction: actor should be stable internal ID + optional human-readable name. Email should not be in the canonical event by default. If any legacy events still include email, docs should say “may include username/display name depending on IdP configuration” but avoid promising email.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Also: do not include full IP if customer has IP anonymization enabled (some enterprises do). We can say “source_ip when available”.

Confluence Bot: Elliot Price shared a link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SEC/PII+Redaction+and+Sensitive+Fields+Policy+for+Audit+Events

Elliot Price: Thanks. I’ll lift the approved language from that page.

Miguel Santos: Can we get a short “customer expectations” section we can paste into support responses?
Like:
- “If downstream is throttling, you’ll see lag; events will arrive later (not lost).”
- “If you rotate credentials, expect up to X min before exporter resumes.”
- “If you change filters, it doesn’t retroactively delete already delivered events.”

Colin O'Donnell: + add: Splunk HEC sometimes returns 200 but drops events later if index misconfigured. We should include “validate by searching for a known event_id” and recommend enabling ACK in Splunk if they insist (though we’re not supporting ACK in beta iirc).

Naomi Feldman: We are not supporting Splunk ACK in beta. Please don’t mention it as a supported mode. You can mention “Splunk-side settings may affect indexing; validate in Search”.

Dylan Brooks: Also: filter semantics doc should clarify evaluation time. If filters are applied in exporter, there may be a short window where old config is still in effect (config sync interval). Don’t promise instant.

Elliot Price: Good call. I’ll add: “config changes typically take effect within a few minutes” and avoid hard numbers.

Elliot Price: Open question: what do we say about delivery guarantees?
Draft language:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery for enabled export destinations. During retries or replay, duplicate events may be delivered. Each event includes a stable `event_id` so downstream systems can deduplicate.”

Marcus Lin: That’s the right shape. Add: “Ordering is not guaranteed across partitions/destinations.” And avoid “exactly-once” anywhere.

Kevin Osei: And add retention/backfill:
- “Redwood retains audit logs for X days (per plan). Export is near-real-time but not a replacement for retention requirements unless customer stores exported data.”
- “Backfill can export recent history up to N days (beta).”

Naomi Feldman: Retention period varies by plan + contract, so we need to be careful. Don’t put a single number unless it’s universally true. Prefer “per your plan/contract” and point to the retention policy doc.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll phrase as “Redwood retention is defined by your plan/contract; export is customer-controlled and customers are responsible for retention in their SIEM/storage.”

Miguel Santos: For beta cohort, customers are going to ask “how far back can we backfill?” We should pick a default statement like “up to 7 days” or “up to 24 hours” so CS isn’t improvising.

Naomi Feldman: For beta: we agreed to allow backfill up to 7 days max. Some tenants may get less depending on retention, but we can safely say “up to 7 days, subject to plan retention.”

Kevin Osei: Works. I’ll incorporate that in the retention doc and the FAQ.

Colin O'Donnell: For Splunk guide I’ll include a section:
- required fields for searching: `event_id`, `category`, `action`, `actor.id`, `resource.type`, `workspace_id`
- recommended Splunk sourcetype: `redwood:audit`
- index: customer choice

Dylan Brooks: Please also capture the Console status fields names so CS can map screenshots to actual UI labels. We’ve got: “Export lag”, “Last success”, “Last error”, “Paused (auto)” vs “Paused (manual)”.

Elliot Price: Can you DM me the exact UI strings once final? I’ll keep docs consistent.

Dylan Brooks: Yep, will do.

Elliot Price: Timeline: I want all docs in review by EOD Thu so we can publish internally before the next beta check-ins.
- Colin: Splunk guide draft by Thu noon?
- Kevin: retention language by Thu EOD?
- Marcus: can you review FAQ + admin docs Fri morning?

Colin O'Donnell: Thu noon doable.

Kevin Osei: Thu EOD ok.

Marcus Lin: Fri morning review ok. Tag me when ready.

Miguel Santos: I’ll start a troubleshooting appendix with placeholders and sync with Colin on Splunk + with someone on Datadog/S3 (I don’t have those failure modes memorized).

Elliot Price: I can add Datadog/S3 common errors based on recent tickets (403 key scope, S3 AccessDenied/KMS). I’ll also link to runbook for deeper triage.

Elliot Price: Action items:
- [Elliot] Draft Console admin docs + FAQ language (delivery guarantees, beta scope) + Datadog/S3 troubleshooting bullets
- [Colin] Splunk HEC internal integration guide (setup + validation + common errors)
- [Miguel] Troubleshooting appendix structure + CS-ready canned responses
- [Kevin] Retention vs export responsibilities doc + backfill wording (“up to 7 days, subject to retention”)
- [Marcus] Security review for PII + semantics + “don’t overpromise” pass

Elliot Price: I’ll post links here once drafts are in Confluence.

Draft language:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery for enabled export destinations. During retries or replay, duplicate events may be delivered. Each event includes a stable `event_id` so downstream systems can deduplicate.”

Marcus Lin: That’s the right shape. Add: “Ordering is not guaranteed across partitions/destinations.” And avoid “exactly-once” anywhere.

Kevin Osei: And add retention/backfill:
- “Redwood retains audit logs for X days (per plan). Export is near-real-time but not a replacement for retention requirements unless customer stores exported data.”
- “Backfill can export recent history up to N days (beta).”

Naomi Feldman: Retention period varies by plan + contract, so we need to be careful. Don’t put a single number unless it’s universally true. Prefer “per your plan/contract” and point to the retention policy doc.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll phrase as “Redwood retention is defined by your plan/contract; export is customer-controlled and customers are responsible for retention in their SIEM/storage.”

Miguel Santos: For beta cohort, customers are going to ask “how far back can we backfill?” We should pick a default statement like “up to 7 days” or “up to 24 hours” so CS isn’t improvising.

Naomi Feldman: For beta: we agreed to allow backfill up to 7 days max. Some tenants may get less depending on retention, but we can safely say “up to 7 days, subject to plan retention.”

Kevin Osei: Works. I’ll incorporate that in the retention doc and the FAQ.

Colin O'Donnell: For Splunk guide I’ll include a section:
- required fields for searching: `event_id`, `category`, `action`, `actor.id`, `resource.type`, `workspace_id`
- recommended Splunk sourcetype: `redwood:audit`
- index: customer choice

Dylan Brooks: Please also capture the Console status fields names so CS can map screenshots to actual UI labels. We’ve got: “Export lag”, “Last success”, “Last error”, “Paused (auto)” vs “Paused (manual)”.

Elliot Price: Can you DM me the exact UI strings once final? I’ll keep docs consistent.

Dylan Brooks: Yep, will do.

Elliot Price: Timeline: I want all docs in review by EOD Thu so we can publish internally before the next beta check-ins.
- Colin: Splunk guide draft by Thu noon?
- Kevin: retention language by Thu EOD?
- Marcus: can you review FAQ + admin docs Fri morning?

Colin O'Donnell: Thu noon doable.

Kevin Osei: Thu EOD ok.

Marcus Lin: Fri morning review ok. Tag me when ready.

Miguel Santos: I’ll start a troubleshooting appendix with placeholders and sync with Colin on Splunk + with someone on Datadog/S3 (I don’t have those failure modes memorized).

Elliot Price: I can add Datadog/S3 common errors based on recent tickets (403 key scope, S3 AccessDenied/KMS). I’ll also link to runbook for deeper triage.

Elliot Price: Action items:
- [Elliot] Draft Console admin docs + FAQ language (delivery guarantees, beta scope) + Datadog/S3 troubleshooting bullets
- [Colin] Splunk HEC internal integration guide (setup + validation + common errors)
- [Miguel] Troubleshooting appendix structure + CS-ready canned responses
- [Kevin] Retention vs export responsibilities doc + backfill wording (“up to 7 days, subject to retention”)
- [Marcus] Security review for PII + semantics + “don’t overpromise” pass

Elliot Price: I’ll post links here once drafts are in Confluence.
text:
Elliot Price: Kicking off docs coordination for SIEM-ready audit export beta. We have a few artifacts in flight (integration guide, Security FAQ, retention/backfill language). Need ownership + review path so we don’t ship contradictory wording.

Elliot Price: Current drafts/targets:
- Internal CS/SE guide: Splunk HEC setup + troubleshooting
- Security FAQ (customer-facing-ish, via Sales enablement)
- Admin docs for Console: “Audit Export Settings” + “Status/Health” + “Test connection”
- Retention vs export responsibilities (GRC language)

Elliot Price: @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei can you confirm who in Security is primary reviewer for wording around (a) PII minimization/redaction and (b) delivery guarantees (at-least-once, dedup expectations)?

Marcus Lin: I can be primary on semantics + PII boundary. Kevin can do retention/backfill + compliance questionnaire language. Also want to make sure we don’t accidentally promise “exactly-once”.

Kevin Osei: +1. Happy to own the “what we retain vs what customer retains” section and any SOC2/ISO phrasing.

Miguel Santos: From CS side: we need a crisp “what to expect in beta” section + troubleshooting decision tree (Splunk 4xx/5xx, Datadog 403/429, S3 access denied). I can own the troubleshooting content if someone gives me the technical bullets.

Colin O'Donnell: I can provide technical bullets + screenshots for Splunk HEC (token, index, sourcetype) and common errors. Also note Splunk HEC will 429 hard; exporter backoff is non-trivial.

Naomi Feldman: Re: “beta limitations must be explicit”:
- Hosted + Dedicated only (no Private/on-prem in beta)
- Backfill window limited (we’re not doing unlimited historical export)
- Delivery is at-least-once; customer should dedup on `event_id`
- Some fields conditional (IP/UA only for certain event types)

Naomi Feldman: We should align on the exact backfill limit that we want to publicly state (and that matches product defaults).

Dylan Brooks: For Console admin docs: I can get you UI copy (empty states + “Test connection” flow). But please don’t document knobs we might hide behind FF (filter builder advanced predicates). We’re trying to keep v1 simple.

Elliot Price: Great. Proposing ownership:
- Console admin docs (how to configure + status): Elliot (with Dylan for UI copy)
- Splunk HEC internal guide (CS/SE): Colin (with Miguel for troubleshooting structure)
- Security FAQ: Elliot to draft, Security to review/sign off
- Retention vs export responsibilities: Kevin (with Elliot to format/publish)

Elliot Price: Review chain suggestion:
1) Eliot drafts -> 2) Security review (Marcus/Kevin) -> 3) CS review (Miguel) -> 4) PM final (Naomi) for scope/roadmap statements.

Marcus Lin: Works. For Security review, I specifically want:
- No raw request/response payloads in examples (avoid customer prompts, completion text)
- Explicit statement: audit export contains metadata about actions, not model I/O
- PII section: “source_ip/user_agent may appear where applicable” + mention redaction rules for actor identifiers (email hashing? depends on schema)

Elliot Price: On that last point: are we hashing emails/user identifiers in audit events? I thought we were storing actor as user_id + display name (if present) but not full email.

Kevin Osei: Current direction: actor should be stable internal ID + optional human-readable name. Email should not be in the canonical event by default. If any legacy events still include email, docs should say “may include username/display name depending on IdP configuration” but avoid promising email.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Also: do not include full IP if customer has IP anonymization enabled (some enterprises do). We can say “source_ip when available”.

Confluence Bot: Elliot Price shared a link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SEC/PII+Redaction+and+Sensitive+Fields+Policy+for+Audit+Events

Elliot Price: Thanks. I’ll lift the approved language from that page.

Miguel Santos: Can we get a short “customer expectations” section we can paste into support responses?
Like:
- “If downstream is throttling, you’ll see lag; events will arrive later (not lost).”
- “If you rotate credentials, expect up to X min before exporter resumes.”
- “If you change filters, it doesn’t retroactively delete already delivered events.”

Colin O'Donnell: + add: Splunk HEC sometimes returns 200 but drops events later if index misconfigured. We should include “validate by searching for a known event_id” and recommend enabling ACK in Splunk if they insist (though we’re not supporting ACK in beta iirc).

Naomi Feldman: We are not supporting Splunk ACK in beta. Please don’t mention it as a supported mode. You can mention “Splunk-side settings may affect indexing; validate in Search”.

Dylan Brooks: Also: filter semantics doc should clarify evaluation time. If filters are applied in exporter, there may be a short window where old config is still in effect (config sync interval). Don’t promise instant.

Elliot Price: Good call. I’ll add: “config changes typically take effect within a few minutes” and avoid hard numbers.

Elliot Price: Open question: what do we say about delivery guarantees?
Draft language:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery for enabled export destinations. During retries or replay, duplicate events may be delivered. Each event includes a stable `event_id` so downstream systems can deduplicate.”

Kevin Osei: And add retention/backfill:

- “Backfill can export recent history up to N days (beta).”

Naomi Feldman: Retention period varies by plan + contract, so we need to be careful. Don’t put a single number unless it’s universally true. Prefer “per your plan/contract” and point to the retention policy doc.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll phrase as “Redwood retention is defined by your plan/contract; export is customer-controlled and customers are responsible for retention in their SIEM/storage.”

Miguel Santos: For beta cohort, customers are going to ask “how far back can we backfill?” We should pick a default statement like “up to 7 days” or “up to 24 hours” so CS isn’t improvising.

Naomi Feldman: For beta: we agreed to allow backfill up to 7 days max. Some tenants may get less depending on retention, but we can safely say “up to 7 days, subject to plan retention.”

Kevin Osei: Works. I’ll incorporate that in the retention doc and the FAQ.

Colin O'Donnell: For Splunk guide I’ll include a section:
- required fields for searching: `event_id`, `category`, `action`, `actor.id`, `resource.type`, `workspace_id`
- recommended Splunk sourcetype: `redwood:audit`
- index: customer choice

Dylan Brooks: Please also capture the Console status fields names so CS can map screenshots to actual UI labels. We’ve got: “Export lag”, “Last success”, “Last error”, “Paused (auto)” vs “Paused (manual)”.

Elliot Price: Can you DM me the exact UI strings once final? I’ll keep docs consistent.

Dylan Brooks: Yep, will do.

Elliot Price: Timeline: I want all docs in review by EOD Thu so we can publish internally before the next beta check-ins.
- Colin: Splunk guide draft by Thu noon?
- Kevin: retention language by Thu EOD?
- Marcus: can you review FAQ + admin docs Fri morning?

Colin O'Donnell: Thu noon doable.

Kevin Osei: Thu EOD ok.

Marcus Lin: Fri morning review ok. Tag me when ready.

Miguel Santos: I’ll start a troubleshooting appendix with placeholders and sync with Colin on Splunk + with someone on Datadog/S3 (I don’t have those failure modes memorized).

Elliot Price: I can add Datadog/S3 common errors based on recent tickets (403 key scope, S3 AccessDenied/KMS). I’ll also link to runbook for deeper triage.

- [Elliot] Draft Console admin docs + FAQ language (delivery guarantees, beta scope) + Datadog/S3 troubleshooting bullets
- [Colin] Splunk HEC internal integration guide (setup + validation + common errors)
- [Miguel] Troubleshooting appendix structure + CS-ready canned responses

source.messages:
Elliot Price: Trying to lock doc structure for Private backup/restore + DR. Question: what goes on customer-facing docs site vs internal Confluence runbooks? Need a clear split so Support/SEs don’t send customers the wrong thing.

Kira Thompson: +1. Right now we’ve got:
- Confluence: overview + runbook-control-plane-backup/restore + DR plans
- GitHub docs PR draft: <https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/812>
But there’s overlap (step-by-step commands in both).

Vivek Kulkarni: From product POV: customers need a “how to” that’s complete enough for self-serve + procurement/security review, but not full internal ops playbooks.
We should have docs site include supported envs, prerequisites, and an end-to-end backup + restore flow.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on not shipping internal runbooks publicly. The Confluence runbooks include on-call assumptions (paging, internal tooling, staging references). Also some “if X breaks, escalate to Y” that we don’t want in public docs.

Markus Klein: From SE side, customers ask for:
- exact what’s backed up (control plane state vs k8s etc)
- where artifacts live (S3/NFS) and how to verify
- restore into new cluster vs in-place
- validation checklist they can run
If docs site has those, we can stop sending Confluence PDFs around.

Marcus Lin: Security constraint: public docs should avoid anything that implies we back up plaintext secrets. Be explicit: backups contain encrypted artifacts + references; secret material is handled via KMS/HSM integrations per deployment. Also include retention + audit evidence section (high-level).

Connor O'Brien: Release angle: docs site should have versioned instructions tied to installer/tooling version. Confluence can be “living runbooks” but the external doc must match what ships. Also, for air-gapped: include signed bundle verification steps, but keep internal signing pipeline troubleshooting in Confluence.

Fatima Noor: For on-prem/air-gapped customers, they *need* a deterministic bundle layout + offline docs. Public docs should cover: required artifacts, checksums/signature verification, and “no outbound calls” behavior. The internal runbook has a bunch of customer-specific gotchas we shouldn’t publish.

Elliot Price: Proposed split (sanity check):

DOCS SITE (customer-facing):
1) Overview page: what this toolkit does, supported deployment types (VPC/on-prem/air-gapped), what is/isn’t backed up.
2) Prereqs: storage backends (S3+KMS, NFS), IAM/KMS requirements, permissions, sizing/retention basics.
3) “How to take a backup” (installer CLI + Helm CronJob config). Include verification (`manifest`, checksums, decrypt test) and where to look for logs.
4) “How to restore” (new cluster + same cluster), bootstrap ordering at a high-level, and post-restore validation steps.
5) DR checklists (single region VPC + on-prem air-gapped) with RPO/RTO guidance (targets + what impacts them).
6) Troubleshooting + FAQ: common errors (RBAC denied, missing CRDs, KMS unavailable), and support boundaries.

CONFLUENCE (internal):
A) Full runbooks with exact commands (including quiesce steps, internal service endpoints, escalation, rollback criteria).
B) SRE smoke test / restore drill automation runbooks.
C) Release + signing pipeline details.
D) Game day planning + postmortem templates.

Does that align with everyone’s expectations?

Sean Gallagher: Looks right. Only tweak: on docs site, validation checklist should be “customer runnable” and not rely on internal dashboards. If we reference metrics, point to what they can see (Prometheus metrics in-cluster, installer validation report).

Kira Thompson: Agree. Also: Confluence can keep the “why” + decision history (ADR links). Customer docs should just say “this is the supported way” and link to config examples.

Marcus Lin: +1, and on docs site we should add a small section: “Encryption model” (envelope encryption, key ownership, rotation expectations, what happens when KMS is down). Keep it non-implementation but precise.

Connor O'Brien: Versioning note: docs site must mention backup format compatibility (e.g., backup created on X can restore on X/Y). If we don’t have that nailed, we should put a conservative statement + link to release notes.

Vivek Kulkarni: Please make sure docs site has an explicit “Out of scope / not covered” section:
- cluster etcd recovery
- full k8s object backup (unless using Velero separately)
- data plane state (stateless assumptions)
We keep getting misaligned expectations.

Markus Klein: +1, and include a one-liner we can paste in emails: “Runbooks exist but are internal; customer guide is here: <docs link>”. Support keeps getting asked for Confluence access.

Fatima Noor: For air-gapped, please include a note on where the offline docs are located in the bundle (path) + how to validate checksums without internet.

Elliot Price: Ok. Action items:
- I’ll update <https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/812> to match the split and remove internal-only steps.
- Kira: can you add a Confluence note at top of each runbook: “internal use only; customer guide is …”?
- Sean: can you review the customer validation checklist for on-call-safe wording?
- Marcus: can you provide 5-6 bullets for the encryption/retention section wording (what we can safely claim)?
- Connor/Fatima: confirm what we can publish about air-gapped signature verification + bundle layout.

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll also add a note: restore drills in non-prod may page only during business hours (don’t want customers thinking we guarantee that behavior).

Marcus Lin: Will do. Please route final wording through security review before publish.

Connor O'Brien: 👍 I’ll comment on PR with the exact verification commands we can publish + what stays internal.

Kira Thompson: Ack, I’ll add the internal-only banner + link. Also will create a short doc outline in Confluence so people stop creating new pages ad hoc.

Vivek Kulkarni: Thanks. Once docs outline is in place, I’ll align Support enablement / customer handout to it.

Elliot Price: Perfect. Goal: have docs site pages stable by end of week so SEs can send to FinBank/MedData without caveats.

source.text:
Nate Brooks: Heads-up: user reported confusing examples for POST /v1/generate. Docs show both `max_tokens` and `max_new_tokens` in different places. We need to pick canonical param and align SDK snippets + OpenAPI examples.
Keiko Tan: Ugh yes. Where are they divergent?
Nate Brooks: I found `max_tokens` in docs/openapi/generate.yml example.request.body, but snippets/js/generate_quickstart.js and python/snippets/generate_example.py use `max_new_tokens`.
Rafael Mendes: Runtime accepts `max_new_tokens` only (legacy `max_tokens` mapped client-side earlier but removed). We should standardize on `max_new_tokens` everywhere and add a migration note.
Hannah Support: Customer ticket #5229 hit this. They passed `max_tokens` and got ignored (no truncation), so they were billed for more tokens than expected. We need to be clear and add a troubleshooting note.
ci-bot: Lint error in PR #1402: docs/openapi/generate.yml example.request.body contains unexpected property `max_tokens` (schema expects `max_new_tokens`).
docs-bot: Also, deploy preview failed: streaming examples use SSE event name `message` while SDK expects `delta` events. Inconsistent event names in examples cause confusion in quickstart.
Keiko Tan: Good catch. Proposed fixes:
- Replace `max_tokens` -> `max_new_tokens` in OpenAPI example and all snippets.
- Add migration blurb: "If you previously used `max_tokens` in examples, migrate to `max_new_tokens`. Server only honors `max_new_tokens`."
- Update streaming example to show SSE events `event: delta` and final `event: done`.
Nate Brooks: Can you also update the JS snippet streaming handler? It currently listens for `message` and `end` events. Minimal patch:```js
const es = new EventSource(url);
es.addEventListener('delta', e => { processDelta(JSON.parse(e.data)) });
es.addEventListener('done', () => { finish(); });
```
Rafael Mendes: I'll grep the repo for `max_tokens` and `event: message` and submit a PR. Also add a short note in the API ref: "Note: `max_tokens` examples are deprecated; prefer `max_new_tokens`."
ci-bot: Reminder: update example schemas in openapi/generate.yml to match the request/response models or the preview will fail.
Keiko Tan: Also, the response example contains `finish_reason: "stop"` but schema defines `stop_reason`. We should standardize on `stop_reason` (the runtime field is `stop_reason`). Small but important for SDK parsing examples.
Hannah Support: Please add a small troubleshooting tip: "If your client ignores max tokens, confirm you're using `max_new_tokens` and not `max_tokens`." I'll paste the customer-facing line in support KB after merge.
Rafael Mendes: Pushed PR branch with changes: replace max_tokens -> max_new_tokens, update SSE event names to `delta`/`done`, and fix response field `finish_reason` -> `stop_reason`. PTAL: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/1403/files
ci-bot: PR #1403 failed lint: openapi/generate.yml example.response contains field `finish_reason` not present in schema (expected `stop_reason`).
Keiko Tan: I forgot to update the response example in OpenAPI to `stop_reason`. I'll push a hotfix. Also added migration note under "Generation -> Parameters" and a one-line entry in the SDK changelog.
Nate Brooks: After you push, run `make docs-lint` locally; CI tends to block on example/schema mismatches.
Rafael Mendes: Hotfix pushed and CI re-run. PTAL.
ci-bot: CI passed for PR #1403. Preview deployed.
docs-bot: Merged. Reminder: follow up to sweep auto-generated examples (we have a generator that might still emit `max_tokens` from legacy templates).
Hannah Support: Thanks team  Customer updated ticket and confirmed the KB note was helpful. I'll monitor for any other tickets. :thumbsup:
Nate Brooks: Great work. Filing a small follow-up to scan generator templates and update any lingering `event: message` or `max_tokens` templates. Rafael, can you own that?
Rafael Mendes: Sure; I'll run grep + replace and open a tiny PR tomorrow.
Keiko Tan: Closing loop: added explicit example blocks for both sync and streaming usage, and a short migration FAQ in the generation page.
Nate Brooks: Nice. Marking this resolved unless support surfaces more edge cases.

source.text:
Ava Chen: Heads up — doc-lint job flagged a couple of broken anchors in the runbook for kv-cache tuning. CI link: https://ci.redwood.internal/4782
Rohit Patel: Which anchors? I saw a 404 when following the 'prefix caching' link earlier.
Ava Chen: Root message: in /kb/runbooks/kv-cache.md, anchors `#prefix-caching` and `#eviction-policies` are 404 from the staging preview. docs-bot posted the failing job output:
docs-bot: doc-lint failing: 2 broken anchors found in /kb/runbooks/kv-cache.md
Sam B.: Pinged the owners listed in frontmatter but no response yet. @Lin Morales can you take a quick look? Should be a tiny fix (add anchors or update links).
Lin Morales: I'll pick this up — sending a PR in a few mins. Quick question: do we prefer adding explicit anchors or updating the links to the subsection slugs? The section headers were reworded in last sprint.
Rohit Patel: If headers changed, updating links is better (keeps semantics). Add a short changelog note in the PR describing the header rename.
Ava Chen: PR link once ready and I'll queue it for a fastpatch merge. Also can we disable the 'block-on-broken-anchor' gate for the docs CI for 30m if this blocks a release? Not ideal but we can fastpatch.
Sam B.: Please don't disable; we can fastpatch and merge if Lin creates the PR quickly. I'll review once it's up. :thumbsup:
Lin Morales: PR opened: https://git.internal/redwood/docs/pull/842 — fixes anchors and updates two links. Small unit tests updated.
docs-bot: CI job started: doc-lint, linkcheck, markdown-lint
Sam B.: Reviewed and left one comment about the changelog line. @Ava Chen — can you give final approve after I resolve the comment?
Ava Chen: LGTM after comment addressed. Thanks @Lin for the quick turnaround. Merging now.
docs-bot: Merge complete. doc-lint now passes on main.
Rohit Patel: Verified staging preview shows the anchors working. Nice!
Ava Chen: Filing a tiny follow-up note in the runbook: 'Note: header slugs changed 2026-03-23 — update internal links accordingly', so future search catches it.
Sam B.: awesome, thanks team. Closing thread.
